Switch sports is barebones, lacks innovation and somehow ends up a lesser game than its predecessors. There's fun to be had in party-settings but the big feature (online) gets grindy very quickly.

Volleyball is very jarring, badmitton is dull and simple, bowling battle-royale style is tedious with a tiny margin for error, soccer is actually the most fun, chumbara (swordfighting) feels very imprecise and **** just wrong. 2V2 is unfair when you don't match with another player (meaning you have to control both players) but the other team has two independent players.

Unlocking cosmetics feels like a grind, and its pointless if every player is choosing from a very small subset of options. You only get slightly more points for winning than losing, so the easiest way to earn points is to lose. There's also no payout if a player leaves a match.

Switch Sports is definitely going to need more than just Golf being added to stay on my shelf. Not being able to earn cosmetics without Switch online kills the single player and makes Switch Sports seem like a way to sell NSO subscriptions.

This is one of the most controversial opinions I have in gaming, but I don't think sticker star is bad. Is it as good as the others? -No, definitely not, but it does still have interesting ideas.

First off this is very great adaptation to fit on the handheld. I feel like this is a fantastic game format for the 3DS.
A lot of people hate the sticker format of combat. I know the criticism, and I get it, but I don't fully agree. The idea is cool, I like the idea that you can collect stickers and attack based off of what you currently have, not just get so over powered that you'll destroy everything. I will say, it is dumb that some bosses are gate kept with particular stickers. The fact that you can't beat a boss off of pure skill and understanding of the sticker and boss is incredibly dumb.

Secondly: The world in this game is very neat. It's all very simple in appearance, but  the puzzles that are in those worlds are great. In general I find them very enjoyable to explore.

Lastly: My largest problem with this game is it's dialogue and humor. It's not funny, or witty, it's kind of boring in narrative, which for a paper Mario game isn't something you like to see

This game is definitely a fall from grace from what made the original Paper Mario games so special. No more companions, well I guess there's one, but she's the most unfunny, annoying character I've traveled with in a video game. I don't even remember her game, just that it begins with a K. There are puzzles, but it hard to call them puzzles as there more just confusing backtracking. I don't mind backtracking in video games, but this game takes it to the extreme level to the point where I feel the need to pull up a walkthrough as I have no idea where the hell I'm supposed to go or be doing. As the first Paper Mario game I played, this was a sour opening to a great series.

Paper Mario: Sticker Star was one of my favourite childhood games. I don't know why, but something about this game fascinated me so I played it again and again. But at some point, and I think it was when I heard about the other Paper Mario games and saw Let's Plays about them, I lost interest in the game over time. When I finally got Color Splash, I completely lost interest in the game. As the years went by, I became more and more critical of myself because I was no longer a small child, which completely changed my entire perspective on the Paper Mario series and I now understood what was happening with Paper Mario and the community surrounding the series. I played Sticker Star for the last time in 2021 and unfortunately found that the game is bad both objectively and from my perspective and I think everyone here knows why. But even though I don`t like the game anymore I can understand it why some people like Sticker Star because I liked it before. I don't know why, but Sticker Star has a unique, charming, really likeable but also homely vibe that I only felt in this game, which is also the only real reason why you might like this game and I respect that absolutely. Hate Sticker Star for really, really, REALLY good reasons, love Sticker Star for the vibe, I'm completely fine with it. But yes, I personally don't like Sticker Star anymore, but I'm still kind of happy that I played the game because my child self had a lot of fun with it and that's what counts most in the end, just having fun playing games...

3,6/10

Now i know that People say that this Game isnt that bad and its just so hated becouse its part of the paper mario series and that if it were its original thing people would like it alot more but i think thats the case. I tried to give this Game a chance three times now over the years and i didnt finish it even once. That is simply becouse the Game fails in every criteria that it can fail in. I guess the Sound track and the Visuals arent that bad but thats not the important part of a video game. Those are the Story and the Gameplay, Lets talk about the Story first. This Game doesent have one. Bowser destroys this Star you go collect the random pieces and then you fight him. Nothing more, nothing less. Good enough for a Jump and Run, but fails entirely as an RPG storyline. But, ok, we want to critic this game without taking the other titels of its series into consideration. Maybe Story is simply not what is what it was going for, how does the Gameplay look. Well its certaintly more creative than the Story so thats a good start. The only Problem being, its fundamentaly flawed. A normal RPG Fighting System works like this: you beat enemies to get stronger so you can defeat the boss and progress. This Games Fighting System works like this: you find Stuff you can either use to just defeat the Boss and progress OR you can use it to fight Enemies that usually give you an equivalent of less stuff as reward then when you went into the fight, So we now have a game that has no Story and Fights that you simply run away from. i must apologize i seem to have gotten the wrong impresion of this Game. i thought it was supposed to be an RPG but it seems like this is actually a walking simulator. None of the NPCs have anything special to say or any personality so dont bother trying to talk to them. The only person in this Game with personality is Kersti and i wish she didnt, She is simply mean, grumpy unlikable, always just complains when i try to help her and has zero redeeming qualities. If you ask me why i like Mario the least out of him, Luigi, Wario and Waluigi, my answer would be that he used his one wish to bring this piece of **** back to life. Most of the Gameplay consist of puzzels that i would have probably never solved without a guide becouse the game never tells you anything. The Game justs throws you in and if you dont know something, well tough luck better walk around every single level in this game one more time before finding the Thing you needed in some level outside of your current world that the game never told you, you needed to go to. Also while on this topic, the game never tells you that the thing stickers have action timings, i guess they just excpect you to spam A during them on instinct? But hey if your doing a playthrough right now and you just dont want to go on anymore, ill tell you how to get the quick secret ending. Go to Gooper Blooper and use the Bowling Ball you just got 5 minutes earlier and time it to excellent against him. (only works on a real device i think). I dont know how you could actually defend this Game. Im not trying to tell you that you cant have any fun with it. If you enjoyed playing this Game then good for you. But I simply cant see how you could defend this Game in an objektive way considering it fails in everything it does.

Do I really need to elaborate? It’s pretty clear how awful this game is. I mentally was not able to finish the final boss because of how soul-draining Sticker Star is. Before crapping on it fully, I do like a lot of the music and it does look pretty nice for a 3DS game. But the combat, oh what the heck happened to the combat?! You use stickers to attack but they’re all a one-time use. So when they’re gone, they are gone for good. Because of that, there’s zero incentive to fight enemies because it’s a waste of stickers and time. You just save them for bosses. Which by the way, most bosses require thing stickers to defeat but you have no idea which stickers will work half the time. So you’ll just end up guessing and wasting even more of your time. Pair that up with a linear level structure, no partners, a lack of any interesting characters or story, and no level of system. This is the most playable unfun game I may have ever experienced in my life and likely ever will.

While I don’t think of it as this absolute cancer that everyone else considers it to be, I cannot deny that Paper Mario: Sticker Star is a terrible game. Paper Mario 64 is fantastic, Thousand-Year Door is a masterpiece, and Super Paper Mario is not the same type of game, but still quite solid for what it is. So…what the hell happened, Nintendo?
Admittedly, there are several redeeming qualities. For a 3DS game, Sticker Star is stunning to look at. It’s also indisputable how amazing the music is. Just listen to the Warm Fuzzy Plains! The writing is also pretty funny at times. I personally love that purple Toad in Decalburg! My big hot take on this game is that if you get used to the combat (which I’ll discuss in a second), the bosses can actually be pretty fun! I’m not even kidding!
However, even with all those positives, it still doesn’t excuse some major negatives. The first problem is the lack of interesting characters and story. The first three Paper Mario games have fantastic plot lines and super fun characters like Goombella, Koops, Vivian, Lady Bow, and Count Bleck, but Sticker Star’s plot is super generic, and Nintendo replaced all of the fun characters with Toads…for some reason. Next is the pacing. There are stupid difficulty spikes all over the place, especially if you don’t understand the last and biggest point: the battle system. Instead of the active take on traditional RPGs like Paper Mario 64 and Thousand-Year Door have, Sticker Star’s battle system has no partners, leveling up with experience, and all attacks are consumable. And no, this isn’t like Mario & Luigi: Partners in Time where all of the special (Bros) attacks are consumable items. In Sticker Star, every single attack is a one-and-done sticker. There aren’t even any basic attacks! Not only does this kill any incentive to fight enemies since they waste stickers and grant no experience, but saving the stickers for bosses isn’t always going to work since they require Thing Stickers to beat, which tend to be really tedious to get!
These mechanics completely killed what could have been a great game and burned the Paper Mario series to the ground until the Thousand-Year Door remake on the Switch. Again, while I think the few positives that there are definitely keep Sticker Star from being a 0/10, it is still a terrible game that I’m not even sure is worth playing even out of curiosity.

Unfortunately this game marks the end of the traditional Paper Mario games, which were known for their unique characters, interesting story and strategical gameplay and the beginning of an era of mediocre videogames.

With this game they took out all of the traits, that made Paper Mario so beloved: The leveldesign is bland and similar to standard levels from the NSMB series. Similar to that, the interesting characters were mostly replaced with nameless Toads, whose individual oneliners are not even worth remembering.
Also fights are not as fun anymore, as you do not gain experience points anymore and badges, partners, counters and many more elements, that made them more strategical, were removed.

This is just the beginning, of what I would have to critisize Paper Mario Sticker Star for. In the end I just cannot understand, why the producers (such as Kensuke Tanabe) would follow through with such flawed gamedesign in the next games.
Also, instead of  completely followng through with their new ideology, they seem to be bringing some of Paper Marios best elements back, but only in an extremely halfhearted way!
(For example the ,,badges´´ or ,,partners´´ in The Origami King, which had almost no significance for thr overall gameplay)

At this point I am not even disappointed anymore by newer Paper Mario games.

To anyone, who read this,

-play Bug Fables! It is a great alternative for Paper Mario, as it builds on the style of the first 2 titles.
-I would recommend, supporting Arlo´s petition for Paper Mario.

Thank you for reading!

The fourth entry in the beloved Paper Mario series, Sticker Star isn't what many wanted out of the series, being unafraid to break the tried and true traditions of its predecessors. But the result turned out quite brilliantly, in my opinion. Stickers provide a new and engaging way to battle, with nearly 100 different types to chain together and experiment with, and some of the puzzles will certainly give you a run for your money (though some of them are perhaps a little *too* tough). The Mushroom Kingdom may perhaps be a little too familiar a setting for longtime fans, particularly to those that enjoyed the more unique settings of previous Paper Mario entries, but make no mistake--its realms are just as vibrant, colorful, and papery as ever, made even more appealing by the addition of 3D visuals. And perhaps most admirably, the game is continually inventive and always finds new ways to impress throughout the lengthy 30+ hour campaign, amid many witty bits of dialogue that'll ensure you're in for a good laugh.

It's inevitable that a game that bucks so many traditions will be controversial, especially as a successor to a classic like The Thousand-Year Door. But despite this, Sticker Star gets it right where it matters, and that's in providing you 30 hours of a joyful and colorful adventure--and one you can take with you, too. It has proven difficult for many fans of the Nintendo 64 and GameCube classics to accept Sticker Star's rather unconventional style, but you've truly never played anything like this before and it's absolutely worth a look. As of the time of writing, the game tends to fetch around $20 on eBay, so you certainly don't have to look far, either.

A hollow shell of the OG Paper Mario games.

Good

- the cardboard and paper themes are really well done, probably the most well done in all of the paper Mario games. The attention to detail when I came to how objects interacted with the elements, like wind, was very well depicted.

- the 3D looks really good.

- peeling stickers off of walls on the floor feels quite satisfying, and putting stickers onto missing parts of the levels also felt quite nice.

- I like how the music transitions into a different remix of the same song when I enter a new world on the map

- the roulette is pretty fun to use, as I got good at the timing. Made the random battles go by much quicker.

Bad

- you don't even need the time block that well. The window to block is so huge that it's really hard to fail it. There's just no challenge when it comes to countering. I'm also really upset at the removal of super guard, because it removes the option of very skilled players to attempt it.

- because there is no XP, the wild enemies became a matter of how well i could Dodge them, because there was absolutely no reason for me to fight them since they offered nothing besides some coins, which I could already find plenty of in the level, and after the level.

- the combat is also very shallow. There are no special timing moves. All the qte's were timing the hammer and timing the jumps. Which is way less diverse than the original two paper Mario games.

- some of the scraps that you have to turn into stickers aren't very obvious as to where you're supposed to use them. And collecting the three tablets that you need to access world 2-5 was also very ambiguous as to their location. It was never made clear that each piece was hidden in a separate world, inside of a chest. The game never stated that I needed to combine tablets, never did they mention that the tablets were scattered across three different worlds, and never did they mention that each piece was hidden in a chest. As a consequence, I was trying to paper eyes random scraps in world 2-5, obviously to know avail. And now my scrapbook was stuck with a bunch of scrap stickers which took up all the space from regular stickers.

- it's completely unintuitive and annoying that you must turn objects into stickers in order to use them against the boss. It basically meant I had to fight the boss one time without the super effective sticker, probably lose, restart, go back to deckleberg, make the object into a sticker, travel to the boss again, then fight him. I felt the game should have just allowed me to use the object without having to turn it into a sticker.

- because battles gained me nothing, I avoided them. But this caused another issue. because I tried to avoid as many battles as I could, there were many puzzle clues that I missed because I was trying to escape the enemy.

- sometimes puzzles require certain stickers to be solved. if you didn't have those stickers, it meant you have to walk all the way back to the entrance, then all the way back to the store to buy the stickers you need, then travel all the way back to where the puzzle was. This was extremely tedious.

- Overly cryptic puzzle: 3-5 random hidden blocks to cross broken bridge

- Overly cryptic puzzle: 3-4 wiggler segment hidden in random bush

- Overly cryptic puzzle 5-5: refrigerator freezes entire volcano?!

- Overly cryptic puzzle 5-5: falling rocks don't create hole in cracked ground except on a single crack. How am I supposed to know that one crack is special?!

- for the log River level, how does running on a raft make it move? The logs are clearly connected, or else they would have drifted apart. and if they are connected, there is no way one can run on the raft to make it move. They should have just given Mario a paddle, which makes way more sense.

- I think the biggest problem with this game is having to convert things into stickers inside of shops. This means that if I encountered puzzles requiring a thing sticker, I would need the thing itself. If I didn't, I'd probably be trying a bunch of different s*** that doesn't work, until I looked at a guide to see the thing that I was missing. At which point, I would have to backtrack to a certain world to get the thing. Then go to a shop to convert the thing into a sticker. Then travel all the way back to the world with the puzzle, and all the way to where the puzzle is actually located. Only then can I apply the thing sticker. This is completely unintuitive backtracking. And is almost impossible to solve if you haven't found that thing when trying to solve the puzzle.

- they removed everything good from the paper Mario series. Partner abilities, star Spirit abilities, partners in general, XP, and an actual hub world. Instead of learning new abilities to open the way to the next world, the progression is made way more linear, and when it isn't, you're forced to trial an error thing stickers to attempt to open the way.

Is Paper Mario: Sticker Star really as bad as people say?

Well in some ways, no. This game has some redeeming qualities that no one seems to talk about, like clever and funny dialogue, extremely good music, and fun boss fights, even though the battle system isn't that good. And that's the main problem with this game. The game revolves around these stickers, which you collect throughout your journey and use for battles. The attacks are really cool, but you can only use them one time then they're gone forever. You almost never have to worry about running out since each level is filled to the brim with them. But despite that, I always avoided fights whenever I could to save stickers for bosses. That's not a good thing.

Like all Paper Mario games, you will have to solve puzzles to progress through the game. While some of the puzzles are brilliant, others are practically impossible without a guide, because they revolve around these "things." "Things" are 3D objects found within the game's 38 levels. Once you collect these "things", you can then go to Decalburg, which is the hub world, to paperize them which will allow you to use them. You can paperize as many "things" as you can, as long as your inventory has the space for them. They can also be used in battles, and some battles require the use of a specific one. But there's no real way to tell which of the 64 "things" you need to use without a guide.

So is this game good? Well if you haven't played a Paper Mario game that was released prior to Paper Mario Sticker Star, you will enjoy this game. But longtime fans of the series will be disappointed that this game is not like its predecessors, with no exp or leveling up, no real reason to battle, and the lack of interesting characters. As for me, the perfect word to describe this game is "ok". It's not bad, but it's far from a magnum opus.

Someone would usually say they played TTYD for its great battle system and unique plot. But someone who plays Sticker Star just has less things to say. The only thing they would probably think of are its decent levels and its charm. TTYD isn’t flawless, as its linearity is a bit annoying, as well as the exploration. But TTYD’s positives are much greater as it had a complex plot, amazing battle system, and lots of creativity. Sticker Star doesn't do that. Paper Mario games usually consist of three things: a good story, a burst of creativity, and a good battle system. These three things are what make Paper Mario great. This is why I still recognize Super as a good game, it had at least two of those things and its positives outweighed its negatives by a large margin. Sticker Star has none of those things, it may have decent level design as well as a healthy dose of charm, but its negatives outweighed the positives. Some may say TTYD fans like me put the new games to an impossible standard, and that we have our nostalgia goggles on too tight. But that is a lie. Bug Fables is a game that shows what Paper Mario should be today, it had a good plot, a great battle system, and creativity shooting through the roof. Overall, it was an amazing game, a good 9.5/10. This was done by a small Indie studio, if they can do something like that, it is not impossible for Nintendo to live up to TTYD’s standards. Sticker Star had the chance to bring the series back from the still great but disappointing Super Paper Mario. But it ended up going to the worst point in the series. Sticker Star can only be enjoyed by a small number of people with certain gaming tastes. But for me Sticker Star isn’t a true Paper Mario, it wasn’t anything like the original three and had nothing in common with them other than it being attached to the Paper Mario franchise. Up to this point the games were all either great or amazing games, but this game tarnished the name of Paper Mario and the series has still not recovered from this. I’m going to be generous and give Paper Mario Sticker Star a 3/10.

The game that started the unfortunate downfall of a once great series, everything that made the first three game's so special is completely erased in this game. Instead of a new deep story with an epic adventure which was present in the original trilogy the plot in this game consists of the same boring overused Bowser kidnapped the princess plot which is literally taken from a New Super Mario Bros game. two more games in this series were released after this game and they are unfortunately just as bad or in some cases even worse than this game and with each new release it is looking less and less possible that this series will ever get a decent title that comes close to resembling the quality and creativity that was present in Paper Mario 64, Thousand Year Door and Super Paper Mario.

Just because it's not the exact same game twice in a row does not mean this game is bad. This broke the mold of the first two original games, and took a new direction for the series, while also taking ideas from the previous games. Just because it's easy doesn't mean its bad, and it has a massive amount of appeal for a new person who wants to get into RPGs, while also being a safe bet for veterans of RPGS.

The jazzy music adds a new spin on Paper Mario, and the Mario series in general. The presentation of the game is easily the best of the series, using arts & crafts as its scenery. The game play itself is simple, but it didn't need to take a big risk, it knew what it needed to be.

The battles themselves are easy, while the boss battles require some thinking behind them, albeit less than there should be. The boss battles are easily the high-light of the games, as the battle system works better than people give it credit for.

Sticker Star is not a terrible game, nor is it the worst game in the series. Comparing this with a completely different game is an unfair criticism, and does not really explain what can be said about this with a deep discussion.

With fun combat and great writing this is another excellent addition to the Paper Mario series. Okay, this game isn't great, but it's not as horrendously bad as it's often made out to be. Yes the battle system isn't great, yes the new characters are non existent, yes the story is bland, but besides that the rest of the game is pretty decent. However, the issue is that it has some critical flaws that hold it back from achieving greatness.

As an RPG, I would rate it about a 6, however this game is NOT an RPG. This game is actually an Action-Adventure game.

As an action adventure game, it actually plays much like BotW in a weird way. The point isn't to mindlessly fight and level up in order to win.

The point is to survive, explore, figure out puzzles, and make it to the next area.

The only major flaw I have with this game is boss battles. They all pretty much end up the same way, spamming big stickers and/or specific stickers. The mini-boss fights can sometimes be fun or challenging.

The writing is awesome, Kirsti reminds me of Spirit Tracks Zelda, and the level design is some of the best in any Paper Mario game.

If you hate this game because it's a bad or mediocre RPG... Try it again with a new mindset. This is an Action-Adventure game.

I think that it has a lot of poor reviews mainly because the ones who complain about this game are just hardcore Paper Mario fans who complain about how this game isn't in the same lines as the others and not one complained about Super Paper Mario even though the combat was complete and utter trash. I digress, this is a really good game and I find it enjoyable.

This game feels like a "New Super Mario Bros." with turn-based battles. Which would be fine if it was an actual RPG. That's right, it's an Action-Adventure game. With linear story, characters and locations, some fans don't even consider this to be a "Paper Mario" game, especially with the extremely flawed battle system that gives you nothing but gold coins and no experience. Now as a "Pick-up-and-play" game, it's not so bad. But for those of you who've stuck with the series even in "Super Paper Mario", you will be disgusted of this. The visuals are cute and "paperish", but that's all this game has to offer that will keep you playing.

Unpopular Opinion time! Sticker Star is a great game. Don't kill me.

Cons:

[Re-edited on November 11th, 2016] People, hate on Sticker Star all you want, but don't hate on it being a normal game, hate on it being a Paper Mario game. If you take Sticker Star as its own self, you can make a great game out of it. Unfortunately, this game screwed many things with the franchise itself yet if they're all taken to different media like a movie or another video game, they can be great things. This game behaves a lot and builds a relationship with the main series. This caused a few things wrong.

The entire Paper Mario franchise builds up in eight different chapters of progressing. Sticker Star took it down to six and worse, it's not even chapters, it's worlds. What is this? A Super Mario game or something? It says Paper Mario on it, not Super Mario. Please get it right. Bowser does not say a single word. Literally, not even a single letter. Not even a dialog box. Alright, we get that you're trying to make it like a platformer more than a RPG, but come on, this is a RPG. You must respect its genre and it's a must. No denies and counters. In the Paper Mario franchise, Mario is the silent one. Make it that way.

Sticker Star does have a story, but it's clichéd and predictable from the start. Mentions of previous Paper Mario characters are featured in this game, but there are no new Paper Mario characters in this game or even returning ones, except for Kersti, which ironically doesn't sound original whatsoever. And whatever you do, get at least some fighting stickers or you're already prepared for a game over. No, you cannot level up either. It's impossible to determine what level Mario is in this game.

The music (I'm looking at you, Supreme Sparkling King; Bowser Battle) is great which is quite ironic to say, but most of them doesn't even fit their respective scenarios. How am I going to fight Bowser for the final battle with some cheesy running music and not some menacing feeling like in the first three games? At least Shadow Queen and Super Dimentio had better final battle themes. Bowser even had one of the most menacing one in the series and ironically, he also have the least menacing one in the series. I can make most of the music fit onto YouTube videos and you could just pretend they didn't even come from this game. If you have read my Ghostbusters (2016) review, I have compared this game to that movie with a note of a reason. They both screwed their beloved franchises loved by many people, except Sticker Star is better at just being a good game than the reboot I would not love to call Ghostbusters.

Onward, character design is very generic. It just rehashes previous Mario characters from already-existing Mario games. I've heard of this phrase before and it describes what this game needs. "Get it right and make it that way. Leave it alone."

Sticker Star is like a near 8/10 if you just make it a game itself, a 5/10 if you make it an RPG, but when you put it onto the Paper Mario series, you disappoint many people, 2/10. I can now see where people are getting the 'Paper Mario 3D Land' title thing from. November 11th is Veterans Day, but what does Veterans Day have to do with the topic anyways? Sticker Star is released on the same day. This Veterans Day, don't think bad, think about veterans... and then you can flame Sticker Star on it being a Paper Mario installment afterwards.

Nintendo took everything we knew about Paper Mario and flipped it on its head. All the fun worlds are now gone and replaced with a world map, endless backtracking, bland and one-dimensional characters (basically all Toads), pointless combat (uses up stickers and gives nothing in return), no humorous characters, and not much of a story. The graphics are definitely nice, but that's about the only good thing I can say about it. I tried to be positive about this game but compared to the previous ones, it's bad.

I am only making this review to compare how absolutely terrible this game was compared to Paper Mario: Color Splash. Take Color Splash and remove all semblance of thought that went into it and you basically get a tl;dr for this game.

Ah paper mario sticker star. Truth be told i was excited when i first got this. I started playing it right when i got it. I kept on wondering "when will i get my first partner" and "when will i level up" And then i figured out, no partners and no leveling system. No charming characters and no funny jokes. Just a bunch of toads and mario doing the cliche save princess peach. I got so bored and so stuck i stopped playing. I tried to go back to it but i was so stuck on a part i could not do it. Theres no point in battling either. No reward but coins. And wanna know what you use coins for? MORE STICKERS! AND YOU USE STICKERS TO BATTLE. I was really disappointed. The gameplay certainly isint bad, But its easy to get stuck and no point in even battling. Battles are easy due to some op stickers as well. So this is just a bad game. And with color splash i think paper mario is a dying series. And i hate to say it. So this is just a bad game.

Ignoring all of the, "I only want TTYD, and nothing else" cries.  I've never played TTYD, only the first paper mario, and super paper mario.  This means that I have fresh perspective and I've played 3 very different paper mario games.  I really have enjoyed this game.  Is it different, **** it ****.  75 critic score is accurate and deserved.  There is no grinding involved which many people don't like, and often you should avoid battles as they tend to use up your resources, but resources aren't hard to come by.  The battle system is refreshing to me.  I've certainly never seen another like it.  The game is on the easy side, battle wise, and there could be more info about what stickers to use when, but if you pay attention to the game world and the dialogue, you can figure it out.

This game is bad, but not as absolutely awful as some have suggested.
The main bad thing about it is that it ditches some of the basic elements of an RPG, while still pretending to be one. I actually quite liked the gimmick of using stickers in battle, and having a certain amount of consumable attacks. However, the sticker interface is clunky and not user-friendly; it can take ages to find the stickers you want to use and sometimes you might find you don't even have the ones you've been looking for for ages. A menu would have been better. Another concern about battles is the fact that you no longer gain EXP at the end of a battle, which means that there is absolutely no incentive to play battles at all unless they are unavoidable. The bosses are quite fun but very easy with the right stickers available and sometimes impossible without them.
Also, the areas are more bland, and lack the original characters that made the first 2 PM games so great, in fact every character is called 'A Toad'. It really wouldn't have been hard to at least give them names or different colored spots, or have female toads. The fact that you are going through a linear level instead of looking around areas to complete quests makes it boring as well.
I mean, it's still an entertaining game. There are small amounts of humor and a few more interesting areas, including a roller coaster queue and a quiz show. The soundtrack is average, and the graphics look good in 3D.
You can find fun in this game. But if you want a game similar to the first 2 Paper Mario games, prepare to be extremely disappointed.

Rating: 5.9

The things I like from the game is the paper environment for Mario, and the decent plot, but as everyone says (and I agree with them), the game lacks original characters. I mean, there's Kerstie, but she is the only original character. Another thing that I didn't like is the worlds. This is Paper Mario, not Super Mario Bros., and the last thing the game can improve on is the combat systtem. Sure, the thing that this game is focused on is stickers, but come on! If you want to try it out, go ahead I'm okay with it, but I didn't enjoy this game as much as I did with Super Paper Mario

This is not a bad game but is very disappointing ggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggg I added a bunch of gas to make the review 150 characters long

Paper Mario Sticker Star took away everything that made the first two Paper Mario games great, and was an immense disappointment.

For starters, it reverted to the simple "Bowser kidnaps Peach" story, and thus lacks the interesting and original stories that previous Paper Mario games had. And even though this generic story could have been executed well, it wasn't. A bland story can still be redeemed by interesting characters, but Sticker Star has none, with the game's cast consisting of Mario, Kersti, Peach, Bowser, and about a million Toads. Yes, that's right. Instead of the game being filled with interesting and unique characters, generic Toads make up the entire supporting cast.

The gameplay has taken a dive as well. The battle system, albeit creative, is extremely irritating and lacks much depth. The lack of exp and leveling up takes away all sense of reward, and has essentially stripped the series of the RPG elements that made it great. In addition, the lack of a connected world has removed nearly all sense of exploration that the series once had. Also, the puzzles are unfairly cryptic, many times requiring some obscure sticker that you get halfway across the gameworld, with no clues leading to it whatsoever. The same applies to boss battles, each of which has a weakness to one thing sticker, and the outcome of the battle will usually depend solely on whether or not you have that sticker.

I'd like to be able to say something good about this game, some way it improved over previous Paper Mario games. But it is a downgrade in every way imaginable. The fact that this game's gameplay style is the new standard for the series makes me want to cry. Paper Mario is dead.

Fans of the Paper Mario series won't be disappointed. Yet another fine entry and it looks amazing on the New 3DS. It's not Super Paper Mario or Thousand Year Door, nor does it pretend to be. It's a new entry in the series and quite enjoyable. Recommended for anyone who has played all the others.

I enjoyed Sticker Star. It's no Thousand Year **** it sure as hell is a good game. Sticker Star had new battle mechanics which actually needed some skill to use. I think that is what threw people off the most.

Paper Mario: Sticker Star breaks the trend of Paper Mario games coming to consoles and Mario & Luigi games coming to handhelds...does it break even more trends than that?

I was a big fan of Super Paper Mario and I probably enjoyed it a bit more than I should, no matter how much things it did differently from Paper Mario (N64) and Thousand-Year Door (GCN). I was a bit hesitant about Sticker Star but it proved to be a decent game. The first difference you'll notice is the addition of a world map. The map makes it easier to get from level to level and it suits a portable adventure. However, this is probably one of the more excusable of Sticker Star's many tradition-breaking gameplay elements.

Mario no longer attacks during battles. Yep, you heard me. Instead, he uses various stickers he finds scattered around the world to use. Hammer stickers allow Mario to use a hammer, fire flower stickers allow Mario to throw fire, and there's much more. However, you can run out of stickers incredibly fast as you'll find yourself fighting enemies more times than you'd like to. It you run out of stickers, it can be pretty hard to get your supply back up. The battle system works great, along with the roulette spinner (it can give you more attacks), I just wish it was a bit more improved.

One of my other main problems with the game is the story. Mario RPGs always have complex and engaging stories, with the possible exception of the first Paper Mario. Super Paper Mario is a fantastic example of great storytelling. Sticker Star's story: Bowser crashes a festival and kidnaps Peach. Mario must collect items in the world. Sounds a lot like Super Mario Galaxy. The story is way too simple for an RPG in my opinion but it doesn't make the gameplay bad or anything. In fact, the gameplay itself is very fluid and nice. At points you may come across things you need to fix which requires a "Thing Sticker"...which brings me to another point.

Thing Stickers are...well, things (fans, trumpets, toy boats, etc.) that can do certain things in battles and in the overworld. Unfortunately, you can run out of Thing Stickers so you will find yourself backtracking to past levels over and over again to find the specific Thing Sticker you need to accomplish a task. Kind of ruins the "portable" experience a bit.

Overall, Paper Mario: Sticker Star isn't a bad game. In fact, I enjoyed **** when you have to use a guide to see where to get Thing Stickers and the story doesn't match an RPG, there's probably something a bit off.
In a paper world, stickers fit right in place. The best part of this RPG is the absence of experience. No more annoying grinding I hated in previous Paper Mario titles.

Being a HUGE fan of the Paper Mario franchise I purchased Sticker Star with a predetermined bias. That being said: after getting used to the sticker feature in terms of battles I grew to enjoy the level of freedom that gave the player. There is some heat of the Super Mario World-esq transportation between levels which inherently takes away from the open world that us Paper Mario fans love. While this is true, its not a game killer. the adventure is still exciting and sometimes with a hint of challenge. I thoroughly enjoyed this game and would play it again.

An amazing game everything that was lost with Super Paper Mario was reborn with Paper Mario Sticker Star. With great level design, amazing music (with Koji Kondo having a very new take on Mario music), great bosses, a decent storyline (that is saved by the characters), and a fantastic battle system. My only complaints are it can be hard to find out what to do at times, there aren't any partners, and Mario never levels up but those are all just nitpicks.

Although it has the best soundtrack in my opinion, it is technically the worst Paper Mario game overall. However, it doesn't mean it's a bad game, it still is a lot of fun and I recommend any newcomers to the series to try out this simple entry first before tackling the other ones.

My favorite installment in the Mario franchise is the paper Mario series. each title rarely eveir disappointed and even if it did it made up for it in other areas. Sticker star was a great game. while it abandoned some of its roots like partners, fp, and badges, sticker star was still fun and I hope to see more out of paper Mario soon. my only real concern is that im not sure how well this game would have done if It weren't for the past 3 games.

Great dialogue and music, enjoyable gameplay. The only thing that annoyed me was I felt you had to look up a guide at times to find out which sticker would be effective or go where, it wasn't really the obvious choice a lot of the time. A great game still, I enjoyed it a lot.

The puzzles are quite hard to figure out but don't worry you will figure them out. When I get to the bosses it is frustrating because I use up most of my stickers. It is a really good adventure game and you need to think about your strategy.

If you're a Paper Mario fan, you're either going to love it or hate. Of course just by looking at my score i loved it. The sticker system, while out of tradition, is fun to use, and great use of resource management. The puzzles are more engaging than in any other title. Not to mention the backtracking is not as dreadful as the previous titles, THANK GOD. My only gripe is the boss battles can be really annoying, forcing you to run away in order for you to find the 'right sticker'. Although I miss the old Paper Mario mechanics, this is certainly worth checking out.
Yep, I'm really enjoying sticking stickers for this Paper Mario sequel. And not just that, this Paper Mario sequel features a new RPG-looking that much like a paper.

I am a big Paper Mario fan and I have kept up with the series since it ever came out. In my opinion I think this is one of the best Paper Mario games in the franchise. I have to admit that some of the RPG elements are gone but to me this game was fantastic with it's game play and fun ways in using different stickers. This game also was also harder than the other game, giving me a challenge on what stickers I need to defeat each enemy. I think this also had great 3D visuals and I loved how this Paper Mario game made the world more paper like than any of the other games in the series. This game is fun and well made and is a great game for the Nintendo 3DS. Long live Paper Mario!

pokemon-legends-z-a.review:
I had low hopes and got a bit surprised. The story is mixed bag, it really has some nice moments like AZ storytelling and the end is hype and emotional. But in between with the royale stuff is mid and uninteresting. The side characters writing is boring and you don’t care at all for them, well I didn’t get interested to any characters except ZA, L and Floette. 

The gameplay or well combat at first feels refreshing and new, but after a while repetitive, clunky and actually pretty boring and you notice the flaws. Path finding is weird for the Pokémon, sometimes they just get stuck or attack the air and stuff. Switching Pokémon feels a bit weird. The best part is that the capture system is nice. It’s like legends Arceus where you don’t have to fight to capture and sneak up and throw the balls directly at them. Also when you defeat a wild Pokémon you get a chance to catch it, instead of the old school red health capture, which I really like.

The main missions are pretty good, repetitive again, but it gets you hooked and want to finish the game. 
The side missions are just boring fetch quests for the most part, or just talk to npc then battle then done. I wish they where better. There are some that stand out, like the free shiny mareep quest. 

Not worth the full price that they sell it for. But I will always fall for the high Pokémon price as Pokémon is like my favorite game series of all time since I was a kid. It breaks my heart that Pokémon is going in a bad direction. Hopefully the next games in this series are going to be good like the older games. And please… no more trade evolutions they ****. Add back link cable…

From what all the negative reviews are saying, it’s clear that a decent amount of people don’t understand what the legends series is going for, and that’s a shame. It isn’t gen 10, it’s meant to be a story/lore focused game with experimental gameplay. That was literally even said in the leaks. If that isn’t for you that’s okay- but the naysayers keep bringing up the confined map and taking place in a single city when the THEME OF THE GAME is community and people from all walks of life protecting a place that is important to them in different ways. A wonderful story that gave me chills at the end. Some of the best characters in the series. Take-chan is doing amazing work with her art and designs and it going unappreciated here honestly brings me disgust. The complaints about the graphics make no sense to me. The loss of more detailed buildings are a bit disappointing but I didn’t realize we were playing games to look at windows. It sounds like these people want Elden Ring But Pokemon or BOTW But Pokemon, and fail to understand pokemon is MEANT to be extremely “cartoony”. I do feel like Legends Arceus had graphics I prefer more- the softer realistic shaders don’t work as well with the style but my god am I glad the saturation and hair textures are fixed from the ScarletViolet art direction disaster. That game is a lot more criticizable. This one ran for me without issues on my Switch 2, and my friends playing on Switch 1 had no issues when I asked them. Also unlike ScarletViolet. The experimental combat is fun too, and I think a lot of people aren’t realizing it is a one-off thing. Apparently the next legends game will have a mechanic similar to pikmin, should the leaks be relevant still. If you don’t care for story or experimental gameplay then whatever thats fine, gen 10 may be more suitable for you when it comes out. This game succeeded in what it was TRYING to do, and if what it was trying wasn’t for you, then that’s no reason to downvote it over purposeful choices. No game is perfect so I give it a 9, the only annoying thing was sometimes collecting enough points to continue on with the story felt monotonous. This is one of the best pokemon games if you have whimsy and joy in your heart and Actually Like Pokemon.

Pokemon is a huge let down these days and its very sad to see. Can they just pretend for once that they make money like they actually do. Misers.

Compared to the previous mainline Switch entries, it's decent! I'm surprised the reviews are that low, especially since the base game is no where as offensive as the DLC. As far as the game goes, graphics are not bad at all, runs perfectly well on the Switch 2 (the bar is low I'm aware), battles are fresh (granted you need to actually interact with the mechanics, this isn't a turn based game) and the story has its moments, especially if you're a lore nerd like me. Music (even if repetitive at times), characters and new Mega designs are mostly good.

As for the bad, my number one gripe is the forced day/night cycle. The fact that it throws you in a loading screen no matter what is super frustrating. The game just loves interrupting you and breaking the flow of the game. The writing is also generally pretty unserious, and just kind of bad at times. The new Megas are fine, but I'll put this in the bad section as well because there are some big misses (the ZA starters are one), some big obvious ones are missing (Talonflame? Aegislash? Goodra?) and frankly, not as much as there should be for a $70 Switch game. The environment is a big one people have complained about and I mostly agree, but I can't say it ruined the game or anything for me. Content-wise, it's slightly on the low-end for me, but again, inoffensive.

Overall, the base game is inoffensive to me, in somewhat positive ways. Frankly, biased because I don't remember the last time a Switch Pokemon game felt that smooth and I'm happy that Megas are back. 6-7/10. DLC is a strong 3/10.

Pokemon Legends ZA dissapoints in many ways, however in my opinion dosent warrant the absolute slander of being renowned as one of the worst games ever made. Starting with the pros, ZAs combat system and gameplay loop are very well refined and polished, where the game can be genuinely addicting to play at times, transitioning from participating in the ZA Royale, taking on the Rogue Mega Evolution Pokemon, and exploring the Wild Zones. The combat system feels refreshing and refined, which is not an easy feat due to this being Pokemons first time fully shifting to real time combat in the main series. As for the many negatives, these can be summarised down to the poorly paced main campaign, sloppy writing, lack of voice acting, lack of detailing in the already small Lumiose City, and the very bare bones and unengaging traversal and platforming. This game feels very behind its time in its narrative presentation, with bloated segments of dialogue with nothing of actual substance. The games narrative can be very tone deaf, and is horribly paced with literally all of this games story being shown in the last 2 hours of the game. The world is not excusable for a 2025 release either, with a clear lack of effort when it comes to the detailing and making Lumiose City feel alive. The game is also a further downgrade compared to Legends Arceus in the traversal department, where Legends ZA only offers you a basic sprint and a not very intuitive glide for vertical movement. The platforming segments are dull and feel like theyre there to purely pad timing, due to the lack of traversal mechanisms. My 6/10 dosent factor in the DLC, as I am frankly not interested in purchasing it at all. The reception has been abysmall, with everyone saying its way worse then even the mediocre base game. I would not recommend paying for this game at full price at all, however it can be a fun enough playthrough if bought second hand.
Not sure what to say but the story stinks, the city is small and empty; its a rather boring reptition of activities and battles. Not happy about it.

This is the worst Pokémon game I’ve ever played. The map is small, and bland, and empty. You can’t enter 99% of the buildings in the city even though the ENTIRE game takes place inside the city. Horrible, repetitive story and gameplay loop, with no compelling characters. They also tried a new realtime battle system, and it’s obvious they didn’t even “try” at all. You can steamroll the whole game with just one Pokémon while standing in one spot, just spamming your attack moves that come off cooldown. No thought. No strategy. Just stand there and mash buttons. It’s completely uninspired and devoid of any creativity. The whole game is devoid of creativity. 

This is the first major Pokémon game that I can’t finish. I dropped it after 4 hours. I’m crushed by how bad this game is and what Pokémon games have become in the modern day. As a long time fan of the franchise for the last 20+ years, it’s been depressing seeing the downward trend in quality of recent Pokémon games. And although I certainly think Sword/Shield and Scarlet/Violet were also pretty disappointing and very flawed, Pokémon Legends: Z-A was a new level of low-effort slop. Sword/Shield and Scarlet/Violet honestly look like Game of the Year contenders in comparison to Legends: Z-A.

I don’t know if the negative reaction to Pokémon Legends: Z-A will finally be enough to make Game Freak wake up and start putting out better games, or if Legends: Z-A made enough money for them not to care. Either way, NO ONE should spend a single penny on this game. And if Game Freak doesn’t learn anything from this release, then the future of Pokémon games looks grim.

Pokémon Legends: Z-A is a game that I genuinely enjoy playing. I like this game, and I keep coming back to it. It was clear that Pokémon was going to release another Legends-style title, and when you do that, you are expected to improve in many areas, introduce new mechanics, and still keep the core identity of the franchise while expanding the story, multiplayer elements, and extra content.Legends: Z-A launched on October 16, 2025, but despite the ambition behind it, this does not really feel like a Pokémon game. Battles are no longer turn-based, and the map is noticeably smaller. I believe the biggest mistake Pokémon made was trying to completely change the combat system for a community that was used to classic, turn-based battles enhanced by new mechanics. Once again, the same problem appears: this does not feel like Pokémon.Without turn-based combat, the game promotes a faster and far less strategic playstyle. You stop thinking about lowering stats, setting up strategies, or carefully choosing your moves. Instead, battles often turn into attack spamming, where being faster matters more than making smart decisions. The game technically gives you tools to play strategically, but the combat system itself does not encourage that approach at all.This leads to another major problem: the pacing of battles. The game is extremely fast, and most fights feel the same. The majority of Pokémon can be defeated in just a few hits. If you stop to think too much about which move to use, your Pokémon will already be knocked out. Status effects like paralysis or sleep feel almost useless, as battles end too quickly for them to **** other major type of battle is against the rampaging Mega Evolutions. These fights last longer and are more challenging, with Pokémon attacking the player directly. However, even if you successfully dodge an attack, your Pokémon will often still get hit because it simply follows you instead of dodging on its own. In Legends: Z-A, getting distracted for just a second can cost you the entire fight.Another issue that hurts strategy is the lack of basic information during battles. You cannot see the type of the Pokémon you are facing unless you already know it or completely stop the action to check the Pokédex. This would have been an easy quality-of-life feature to include, but it is missing.I also dislike how important battles feel rushed and less exciting. Even though the game tries to make combat feel fresher, it ends up losing the tension and excitement that Pokémon battles usually have.Visually, the game repeats mistakes from previous entries. Buildings look flat and lifeless, almost like a model or a diorama, which clashes heavily with their interiors that are far more detailed and visually appealing. Main characters look great, but NPCs have a very poor visual style. They do not look like they belong in the same game as the main cast, and many of them look nearly identical. This mix of styles makes the world feel unnatural and sometimes even **** of the main mechanics of the game is the Z-A ranking system, where you start at rank Z and work your way up to rank A through nightly battles that reward points and eventually unlock promotion matches. The idea itself is excellent. However, when you reach rank U, the game suddenly boosts you all the way up to rank F with a weak narrative justification that completely breaks the pacing of both the story and character progression. Since this is the main mechanic of the game and even referenced in the title, it could have been handled much better. Progression could have depended on performance, or players could have started at a higher rank if they performed well early on, but instead the game chooses an abrupt and unnatural shortcut.Exploration is another weak point. Everything in Legends: Z-A takes place in a small city, but it is not truly open. Even when you explore rooftops, everything looks the same and offers nothing beyond generic items. Wild areas are very small, and when fighting wild Pokémon, groups often attack both the player and the Pokémon at the same time. Since you can only send out one Pokémon, the best option is often to run away. There are no distinctive biomes or meaningful differences between wild areas, and important places are mostly shops or Pokémon Centers that all look identical. Side quests are a repetitive filler, completely limited to battling or delivering Pokémon to NPCs.Despite all of these issues, I will continue playing the game. The story is very interesting, and discovering new evolutions and Mega Evolutions is still one of the most exciting parts of the game.
While definitely having its flaws, za runs great, has a really good soundtrack, and the pokemon! THE POKEMON HAVE SO MUCH PERSONALITY

As a long time Pokémon fan, I can easily say that Legends ZA is without a doubt the worst game in the series. The base game has a ton of problems such as the main gameplay loop being nothing more than a repetitive grindfest to gather up points for the ZA Royale an fight your next oppent. It got old real fast and I was happy when the story lets you skip several ranks, almost as if the devs knew no one wanted to do this 20+ times or that they couldn't stretch the story out for that long. As for the visuals, I don't think the graphics are as bad as everyone makes them out to be, but they're definitely not impressive by any means. Don't even get me started on how bland Lumiose City looks with so many buildings looking like they were copy and pasted. On top of that, the city in general feels lifeless with a lot of static NPCs and not a lot going on. Traversing throughout Lumiose is not much better either since your only option to go fast is running with no option to ride Pokémon or a bike even though litteraly every other game has this feature. The Wild Zones are easily some of the worst parts of this games with how tight and compact they feel compared to the openness of Legends Arceus and Scarlet & Violet. At least catching Pokémon is the same as in Legends Arceus, but it feels a lot more restrictive and challenging since there's not a lot of space to work with and when you're trying to sneak up on something, good luck, because there's almost nothing to hide behind and you get spotted almost immediately by the wild Pokémon and the same applies to the trainers in the ZA Royale. The new battling mechanic at first felt like a breath of fresh air that Pokémon desperately needed, but by the end, it just felt like a clunky and spamming mess that barely works half the time. And with how the Wild and Battle Zones are layed out, this makes battles more frustrating since your Pokémon can get stuck between spots with how narrow some areas are or, my favorite, they fall off roofs because they choose some odd paths to the opposing Pokémon instead of just going straight. As for side content, it doesn't fare much better either. There are over 100 side quests which might sound impressive until you realize most of them are just fetch quests or you just battle a trainer and hardly any of these quests last longer than 2 minutes at most. These side quests are the definition of quanity over quality that only serves to pad out playtime and barely add any lore or world building. Easily the worst feature of the entire game is Mable's stupid research tasks which are nothing more than grinding and repetitive challenges that go on forever. A lot of these tasks require you to do multiples of the same thing such as catch Pokémon of particular types, destroy certain amounts of crystals, etc. You have to do these multiple times and it gets really boring. However, that's not even the worst part as one of them requires you to spend more money on Switch Online or have a friend to trade with, effectively paywalling the ability to complete the tasks. The worst task, without a doubt, is to complete 1000 battles which means you'll have to spend who knows how much time in the ZA Royale just grinding the same trainers and I already told you how much that **** on it's own. You have to do all of that blue haired demon's quest to unlock the Shiny Charm. As a game completionist, I didn't even bother doing these since I know they're not worth my time and effort and I didn't even care to get the Shiny Charm this time around. Seriously, f*ck Mable and her tasks. With that aside, my other gripe with this game is the story. As a sequel to X&Y, Legends ZA does nothing to build on the lore of its predecessor. Hardly any X&Y characters return, and the ones that do are completely wasted or underutilized. The new characters aren't better either as most of them are boring and unforgettable. There's no villain or antagonist, giving the story less stakes. And in general, the story is poorly written with terrible and cringey dialogue with no ability to skip them whatsoever. To wrap things up, I wanted to touch on the DLC and how it made the game even worse by doubling down on it's worst component, the grinding. It is cranked up to 100 with a lot of RNG involved. By the time I beat the main story of the DLC, I immediately uninstalled the game and didn't even care to see what else what's in store. That's how bad it was. Anyway, as stated in the beginning, I'm a longtime Pokémon fan and I want nothing but the best for this franchise, but Legends ZA let me down like no other game has before. Because of that, I don't have much faith left in the Pokémon franchise and I think it might be best to put it down for good.

What makes Pokémon Legends: Z-A most interesting is its new battle system: it shifts Pokémon battles from the traditional turn-based format to an RPG style with some action elements. But you can’t make such a major overhaul to the combat system and then expect players to constantly grind battles—especially when, to progress the story, you’re forced to repeatedly farm Apex Battles. Game Freak uses a lot of repetitive loops to paper over the fact that the game itself simply doesn’t have enough overall **** honestly, I really can’t agree with how the characters are written in this game—especially the rival, whose characterization is downright terrible.That said, the strengths are clear: this game arguably has the best presentation and cinematic staging the Pokémon series has ever had. Also, the multiplayer battles in this entry are pretty fun. As for the story, while a few characters aren’t handled well, I still think Z-A’s story is stronger than Pokémon Legends: Arceus and Pokémon Sword and Shield. And because this game ties closely into Pokémon X and Y, it basically feels like an epilogue to X/Y—unlike Arceus, which has a strong “spin-off” vibe. From a story perspective alone, I’d even say you can treat Z-A as a mainline **** in all, I’m still willing to recommend Z-A to players who have already played mainline Pokémon games, because I genuinely find its battle system really interesting, and the story is decent. But if you’re new to Pokémon, you should play Pokémon Scarlet and Violet instead.

The difficulty is basically non-existent or more accurately, it’s so easy that I could just brainlessly spam moves like a total brain-dead player and still win. Because of that, any real strategic depth just went out the window. And don't even get me started on some of the Mega designs... I honestly couldn't stand them. Some look so bad it’s hilarious, like they were modeled by some freshman design student or something.That being said, as a long-time fan, I’m pretty used to this stuff by now, so I still found it acceptable enough to push through until the end.Speaking of the ending though, the "power of friendship" trope and the overall game logic were a bit too over-the-top and exaggerated for my taste. All in all, I’d probably give it a 6 or 7 out of 10, which feels about right for someone as easy-going as me.

I wanted to enjoy this game, and for a little while (once you get past the extremely long, hand holding tutorial, I'm talking 1-2 hours) it was fun, but it gets old FAST,and once you notice the issues they become glaringly obvious, making you how much you spent, and who you gave your money to if they care so little about quality.

The good:
-Pokemon models are charming and colorful.
-The concept of the world building is interesting,or was to me.
-non turn based combat mixes it up a bit (though I never played Arceus so I'm not sure how it compares to the combat there)

The not so good:
-the city is tiny and extremely repetitive. The buildings function more as blocks walling you in, you can't enter many buildings, and the textures are pasted on (badly in many cases, texture and color not lining up, windows and pillars are flat images like wallpaper on cardboard cut out squares keeping you inside with very little immersion,can't unsee this, hard to not notice.)
- the music. I have always loved pokemon music, but why is the entire city (which is the whole map) one single song wherever you go? Day, night, all the same song on repeat. Why not different sections of the city with different tracks to make it feel more alive?
-all the box's are standing still, or walking a few steps going no where. No one eats, or battles, or does anything, they just standin idle mode. I feel like I'm play a very early access game.

-locking some of the mega evolutions behind paid dlc that was announced before the game even came out feels like you're devaluing the original game without extra dlc, and for what? Even more money? If that content is ready why not put it in the base game. Dlc used to be extra optional content, not pieces of base game sectioned off with its own price tag.

-almost all shops are just display menus, you can't go in.

-parkour and climbing onto rooftops sounds fun as a concept, as a concept this game sounds fun in many ways, but in reality they have let it fall flat. Every roof is just flat sections of copy pasted roof images, and the parkour is just random boxes and ladders everywhere. You climb or roll, no jumping, does not feel intuitive.

-you can't pet your pokemon, or play with them, they're just assets, no personality. 

I feel like at this point they're selling the brand and concept of pokemon, the games themselves have been losing heart for years now. Very sad to see.
Don't waste your money, I reccomend waiting for another release, see if they are at least willing to put 3d assets on buildings.

As a game developer myself i know that a player shouldn't be expected to get emotionally invested regarding the struggles a team of devs have to go through in order to deliver their final product to the masses, especially if its a 70 bucks game + DLC + online subscription to complete the pokédex, but i think more and more people are starting to put the suits who decide these things and the people who actually have to code the game in the same camp, and that's really not a productive conclusion for anyone other than losers on Twitter whose entire life depends on bandwagoning any negative trend they come across on their feed.Videogame budgets have been ballooning to the point where they're barely sustainable in AAA projects without DLCs, lootboxes and all of that nonsense that seems to have become accepted if not celebrated among mentally disabled people, just for the sake of adding increasingly less impressive technical improvements. We are way past the era in which tech and money getting lavishly thrown at a project was something that could actually make it better.Even so, GTA 4 back in the day was estimated to have a budget of around 45 million dollars. in 2008. 13 millions, less than a third of that (not accounting for inflation), is just not enough money to create a functioning open world game.Which is also why i think it's not fair to directly compare the developers of ZA to those who worked on Silksong like many have been doing. I can understand making the point that you're getting considerably better value for the 20 bucks Silksong asks for (i haven't played it yet but i'm gonna assume that's very much the case), but the amount of manpower required to create an open world game as opposed to a 2D metroidvania isn't even comparable, 3 people can make a game like Silksong on a shoestring budget just fine as long as they know what they're doing and they can take all the time they need, no team will be able to make a game like ZA look good on a 13 million dollar budget and a tight schedule imposed by cold corporate overlords.What i'm trying to get at is: Legends ZA is ****. Yes. But there is absolutely no way i can buy that the developers were put in a position to create anything other than the absolute bare minimum and i don't think Game Freak themselves should be blamed for the final result, especially when it comes to optimization. Optimizing games is beyond grueling and a pain in the ****, and it's usually the kind of thing that's kept for last, once everything else has been taken care of. They didn't have the privilege of being able to take care of everything else, i promise you.
