Have you heard about the new Pokémon game? Pokémon Legends: Z-A. And it’s an absolute disaster. After waiting for so many years for the kind of Pokémon game fans have been asking for, we end up with an extremely poor product, one where not even the visual quality does it any justice. It’s a boring, restrictive game, with no real animations, nothing meaningful to do, set in a city that is also small. And the only aspect that was supposed to be revolutionary—the change to a new combat system instead of classic turn-based battles—ends up being a worse version of turn-based combat, without adding anything new or genuinely good.

I've been a die-hard fan for over 20 years. I've played every game from Red/Blue all the way up to Scarlet/Violet. Legends ZA is the first time I've ever set a Pokémon game down. 

Unlike most people, the graphics and flat textures didn't bug me much. My issue is moreso with the general world design and story. Lumiose City lacks personality. As you progress through the game, you start to realize that the city blends together and each new area you gain access to feels the same as the last. Compare this to any of the GB/DS era games where each city in its region has its own character and reason to be there, and you'll quickly realize how much Game Freak's region design has fallen off in recent entries. My desire to explore quickly fell off, only branching out to find new Wild Areas so I could complete my dex for Home.

The story felt repetitive after a certain point. Visit Wild Areas to build your dex during the day, compete in Battle Zones to earn your Challenger's Ticket at night, repeat. Real-time-battles were a neat addition (when it works), but the novelty of this change alone wasn't enough to keep my enthusiasm up. Finding out that I needed to win 1,000 of these battles in order to obtain the Shiny Charm felt like an unfair and daunting task. Mix in frequent (and often cringeworthy) unskippable dialogue, and you've got a main story that feels like a chore at times to complete.

I had a bad taste put in my mouth minutes into playing the DLC. After previously grinding out battles and only being around 100 away from obtaining the Shiny Charm, I instead was able to get it from simply registering 5 Pokémon in the new dex. Knowing I'll never get the hours spent battling for no reason back feels like a slap in the face. The DLC's story shared the same repetitive nature the main story had. Make some donuts, catch Pokémon, fight a Mega, unskippable dialogue, repeat. A few hours into the DLC, I gave up. I realized I was no longer having fun, and that's the entire reason I play these games in the first place.

With each new entry in the franchise since Black and White feeling progressively worse than the last, I hope we'll see a day where Game Freak cares about quality again. I wish they would either devote the same amount of care to their 3D games as they did their 2D games, or abandon 3D completely. No one should pay $100 for this level of mediocrity, when not that long ago we were receiving masterpieces for $40. Pokémon as a franchise means so much to me and has for the majority of my life, I just want to see them give me a reason to want to pick it up again in the future.

tl;dr : one single town to explore with no varying locales, a story with almost no content drip-fed in between a gameplay loop stretched to the limits of what you'd call thin, no voice acting, a "new" gameplay for battles that's essentially the bare minimum of what you could do to do away with the traditional turn-based system. All for an asking price of 70 bucks.

------------------------------------------

As is usually the case with this franchise, the game can be summed up with "Lots of potential, barely any of it exploited."

The game has a grand total of One (1) town to explore. That's it. No routes, no other towns, no islands, no nothing. The one Paris knock-off is all you get. Hope you like paved streets and PNG buildings because that's all you're going to see!

This makes progressing through the game's "story" incredibly dull. You see the same locales ad nauseam, regardless of what "quest" you're doing. It got so bad that at one point the game gives you a sewer level and I was ELATED. Elated. To see. A sewer level. Just because it was something, ANYTHING other than god. damn. Lumiose.Speaking of the "story", yes, with quotes, it's honestly... pretty basic, and extremely wafer-thin. You're drip-fed minuscule pieces of it in between the gameplay loop of grinding for a challenger ticket, ranking up in the Royale, and fighting rogue Megas. Three things that you will be spending 99% of your game time doing, which very quickly get old. Also, in typical most-profitable-entertainment-franchise-in-the-entire-world fashion, there is no voice acting. Because of course the little multi-billion dollar corporation couldn't possibly afford voice actors. Of course, old school 2D Pokémon game didn't need voice acting, but we're in 2025, the game is in full 3D, and you're trying to sell me a pseudo-RPG story... with mute 3D character models silently flapping their lips..? And you're asking 70 EUROS for it?

And that's honestly the crux of the issue here. Forget this being a Pokemon game for a second. Imagine this was some indie AA RPG, sold for... 20 or 30 bucks. That would honestly be a decent enough time-killer. But that's not the case. This is a first party game, supposed to be a system seller, of the MOST PROFITABLE ENTERTAINMENT FRANCHISE EVER MADE, sold at FULL PRICE. It's just ridiculous.

Oh, and the combat... I appreciate GF finally deciding to modernize the tired old gameplay formula of turn-based combat, but... this needs work, man. And a lot of it. The new combat system essentially boils down to "spam your moves whenever they're off cooldown". Forget coming up with clever strategies with non-damaging moves. None of it matters here. Just slap on the hardest hitting moves you've got and go to town.

Finally, the multiplayer. It's just sad. You get one PvP mode, which is a free-for-all where 4 players are pitting in an arena against each other. Predictably, the strategy is just securing last hits on the others when they're fighting each other, or just one-shotting everything when you have the type advantage. Primo, Boring. Stuff.

And as for trading... All you get is a code system. You input a number, and you get matched with random people using the same code. That's it. You can't post ads for what you're offering and seeking. It's pathetic. You'll have to go on forums or look up youtube videos of popular communities that dedicate specific numbers for specific Pokémon. Have fun.

Now I'd like to dedicate a little segment to what's good about the game, but I'm genuinely coming up blank. I guess every Pokemon having a 3D model and being able to walk around with you is nice, but it's hardly new.

Anyway, I hate metacritic's complete lack of formatting making these reviews barely legible, so I'll stop here. Bad game, not worth the price in a million years. Still waiting for this franchise to change hands for someone to finally do something actually good with it.

Very disappointing. Save your money and hope they do better next time.

The bad first, since there's plenty of it to go around.
- The battle system is extremely clunky. There are clear moves you should use instead of others since pretty much any of the "Ranged" moves require the pokemon walk over to you in the middle of the fight, wasting plenty of precious time as is, to cast it from where you hit the button. It being made for controllers also means swapping between using balls and battling isn't very responsive and the game doesn't even swap off the ball menu half the time when you fail to catch something, so you just take free damage. Not to mention that for some reason the rogue megas just target your character consistently instead of the pokemon they're supposed to be battling. An ambitious attempt, to be sure, but not well done and a new idea done poorly is as bad as any other bad idea.
- The textures and visuals are the same as always. Gamefreak being gamefreak.
- Voice acting still missing, but that's clearly intentional at this point.
- The wild zones are interesting, but ultimately lackluster. The first few show off pretty well what can be done with them, and instead you end up with a mess of halfhearted ideas for them.
- Returning to trade evos in what's supposed to be the single player rpg style line of games just shows they're being pretty greedy about paid online again and think it'll get people paying for it. To their credit, you can largely catch everything still if you shell out for the dlc apparently, but that's not really all that far in their favor considering they paywalled it.
- Very disappointing selection of pokemon for the base game, but that's largely taste based so I'm only including it as the last of the bad ones off the top of my head.

The good, what little there is.
- Models are at least more expressive than they used to be. There were a few times I could actually see what they were trying to express from a character.
- The cast is all fairly interesting for a pokemon game, which surprised me quite a bit to be honest.
- It does feel a lot more like pokemon have integrated into everyday life in this one, which is a huge step up over most games. They're supposed to be peoples partners, but most games don't really lean into it like this one does. People actually seem to care what their pokemon want in this one.
- The new megas are at least funny, if nothing else. Looking at you, Starmie.

So, as always. One step forward, two steps back in classic gamefreak/tpc fashion.

Battling is fun. The world is classic Game Freak crunch. No textures. Pedestrians stand still in a bustling city. The shops are menus. Why bother creating an overworld? This game could have been a menu. 

Story wise, the team is annoying. You do all the heavy lifting and your rival is incredibly self-centered despite being your teammate.

There are games that have a lot of dialogue, but put effort in the story and animations, as well as present to you with interestint or at the very least, relatable characters.

There are games that don't have much content and get repetitive, but at least have out effort into polishing every square inch of the experience.

There are games that are expensive, but at the very least look great and show that the effort and value have gone somewhere.

There are those that respect your time and don't waste it with meandering "plot" and "dialogue."

This game takes the worst aspect in every field and amps it up to 11 to the point that it feels like a parody of what not to do in a game. It is incredible how little I can care about a story and how disrespected I have felt while playing this... Thing.

Like many other Pokémon fans, I really wanted to like this game, but it fell flat in so many disappointing ways. I think they had some great ideas, but somehow almost every part of the game just felt unfinished. For what is supposed to be a AAA title from one of the biggest gaming studios, this was not acceptable for the price they demanded.I liked the switch-up of the battle system, but I think they needed to push it further. Even taking the cooldowns into consideration, it still doesn’t give much reason to pick any movesets you wouldn’t have picked in the other games. You’re still just choosing moves that deal high damage and have type advantage. I wish there were more combo potential, but every move feels sluggish to use because of the weird delay between calling out a move and the Pokémon actually performing it, along with whatever wind-up animation is required. I also feel that trying to access the item menu during battle feels clunky. Maybe a little more user feedback when you try to open the menu and can’t—idk, it just needs improvement.I liked the creative approach of making an open-world game set inside a big city, but what an absolutely boring and repetitive city this is. Everything looks the same and feels too pristine, in a doctor’s-office kind of way. Textures are repeated everywhere, and if you don’t have a Switch 2, good luck dealing with the pixelation in the ground tiles throughout the entire city.I liked the idea of jumping puzzles, but again, these felt rushed. Why couldn’t our character just be able to jump? I really don’t care for the lack of mobility in an “open-world” setting.Lastly, and what really drove me to write this review, is the stupidly horrible storytelling and writing. I get that this is intended for kids, but give them at least a little more credit. The premise starts off on an unrealistic note and just keeps spiraling downward. I will give the game credit for giving all the characters personality, but it ultimately went to waste. I was here for the Z-to-A grind, but somehow they made even that awkward after a certain point (I won’t spoil anything). Near the end of the game, I was just spamming the A button, trying to get these cringey scenes over with so I could be **** yeah, I wanted this to be a great game—I really did—but Game Freak and Nintendo dropped the ball MEGA hard on this one.
Worst pokemon ever. Would never buy anything from nintendo again after this one

I had some hype for this game because Arceus (which I played for at least 60 hours) is my current favorite Pokémon game,and looking at some trailers I thought that the battle system looked pretty fun but after I played I was so wrong.

First, I need to mention how the graphics look straight out of the PS3 era — like Sonic 2006 — and you can see that from the screenshots I’m showing you. And worst of all, the game doesn’t even run properly on the Switch 1. It barely holds 30 FPS. Arceus, even though it was ugly as hell, at least ran smoothly at 30 FPS. There are already tons of videos showing ugly PNG textures and how especially the environments in this game are terribly made.

Now, let’s talk about the main flaw — there’s absolutely no gameplay variety. What you do in the first hour of the tutorial is exactly what you’ll repeat for the entire game. In the tutorial, you basically learn that you’ll catch Pokémon, complete some side quests, and at night fight trainers in what they call the ‘Battle Zone.’ I can tolerate games with limited variety, but not when the little variety they have is this boring.

Why is it boring, you ask? The new battle system (which is no longer turn-based) just doesn’t work for Pokémon. In every fight, you’ll probably just spam your strongest move (using type weaknesses like always), and then use weaker ones only so you don’t ‘lose your turn’ while waiting for the cooldown. Eventually, you realize there’s no real strategy — you just button-mash your way to victory. 

See now how this system is and clair obscur 33 we can agree that the second choice is a better choice by far.

The capture is equal to arceus so i have any kind of bad things to say about that.

Besides that, there aren’t any real quality-of-life improvements. Every night, when the Battle Zone starts, you have to go through the same sequence — and you can’t skip it. Also, every time you heal your Pokémon at the Poké Center, you have to sit through the full animation. And don’t even get me started on how many black screens you’ll see during cutscenes. The game seriously needs to feel more fluid. I just want to battle trainers without constant black screens — am I asking too much? Or is this the bare minimum for a franchise as big as Pokémon?

On top of that, there’s no bicycle or skates to improve your mobility. The only thing you get is a double jump that **** so bad I don’t even have words for it. Honestly, they deserve an award for managing to make something this bad.

The last negative point I want to mention is how underused the moves are outside of battle. They show a few areas that you can only access with Rock Smash, but they never build on that idea. They could’ve used Surf, Waterfall, or Fly to make you curious about exploring the world — but there’s zero incentive to do so.

To be fair, there are some things worth praising. The music is good (even if it repeats the main theme too often), the character designs are nice and most of them are pretty cool, and the story is actually decent — especially when compared to Sword/Shield or Scarlet/Violet.
Well this not the best Pokemon ever made, but it has clearly lots of potential for fun, and is at least interesting, not gonna talk about the dlc tho....

This donuts mechanic is terrible,have time to do the quests is trash.
New megas is OK, nothing awesome.

Like come on. This is a lazy, unfinished, boring, repetitive game. I can't believe they can charge $70 for a game like this. The open world is really small and underwhelming. The buildings use 2d textures for the balcony and windows and you can't even go inside the buildings in the city! The new combat system wasn't really that great. Combined with the online it is just a mess. The strad is to run around in circles and steal kills. Everyone uses the same exact team when in the regular pokemon games teams are very diverse. The story wasn't that good either. Hell, there wasn't even a bad guy. And if you don't have Nintendo online you are going to have to pay $20 if you want the Kalos starter megas. Also they announced $30 DLC  for the $70 game. That's $120 if you wanna complete the dex. This is just pure greed! The side quests also **** booty. Barely any of them are unique at all! Side quests are supposed to be unique challenges that are fun to do. Instead they are are all either collecting an item or "find this pokemon" and they are insanely easy. The map only takes around 7 minutes to go from point A to point B. And the NPCs just stand there. Like it is a city, and they are just standing still like they are just copy and pasted models that were never coded to move. Even Scarlet and Violet had moving NPCs! The buildings are also just copy and paste with no variety at all! The starter choices also make no sense whatsoever. 2 Johto starters and Tepig? Like this is just odd and doesn't follow the rules that Legends Arceus set. Can you even call ZA a legends game at this point? With the city being so small, unfinished and boring to move across, the starter choice makes no sense, and Legends ZA isn't even that far away in the timeline. And they had a lot of potential to do a legends game for Kalos! It could have given us more lore about Zygarde, we could have seen what Kalos looked like back then, and we could have seen the war in action! That makes me feel beyond dissapointed and I am starting to lose hope in Pokemon all together with the recent games not getting any better. In my opinion, this game is definitly not worth getting.

I don't know how they moved so far forward with Legends Arceus and then so far back with this game. I had some hopes for this game but it just ****. Also, selling cut content 2 months after the game released is wild.

An indirect follow up to one of the weakest games in the series, this title had a lot of lost time (and story) to make up for. What was delivered was a shallow identity crisis for a series that’s been struggling. Thrown into This world with the illusion of choice, with no narrative weight or context your character is the “chosen one” destined to save the city for some nebulous issue that it refuses to tell you about until later in the game. The combat, while a fresh take, continues this illusion of choice and control- instead boiling down to the same formula the series has been trying to steer away from. Great character design is painfully juxtaposed against repetitive and mundane graphics that don’t lend themselves to the fantastical world the game is trying to sell you on. All the factors had me stopping the game four hours in to play a different jrpg that had better narrative weight and gameplay.

Pokémon Legends: Z.A delivers above-average gameplay for its genre. The combat system is dynamic and responsive, making battles fun and encouraging experimentation. Exploration is also enjoyable and successfully conveys the sense of freedom and novelty that the Legends series aims to provide. The soundtrack is another highlight, adding charm and atmosphere to the adventure in Lumiose City.Unfortunately, the game suffers heavily on a technical level. For a title developed for a new-generation console—especially considering the potential of the Nintendo Switch 2—the visuals are extremely disappointing. Buildings look flat and lack proper geometry, often resembling low-quality PNG images, which severely hurts immersion.Many assets, including Pokémon and environmental objects, feel graphically outdated, comparable to PlayStation 2 or early PlayStation 3-era games. This technical shortcoming overshadows what could have been a major step forward for the **** absence of voice acting is also a major drawback. Nintendo, Game Freak, and Creatures Inc. once again fail to meet modern industry standards by not offering voiced characters, at least in English and Japanese. Additionally, the lack of Brazilian Portuguese subtitles is frustrating, especially considering Brazil is one of Pokémon’s largest audiences.Overall, Pokémon Legends: Z.A has solid ideas, engaging gameplay, and a strong soundtrack, but is held back by outdated technical choices and questionable production decisions. It feels like a missed opportunity to fully realize both the game’s and the console’s potential.

Extremely medicore, I really thought they had kind of a comeback with Arceus and to a lesser extend with SV. The combat is kind of fun in a flashy and time saving kind of way, but even that barely works. The city setting could work if we had much better visual variety. I enjoy online combat, even if it's not even close to as engaging as the usual. If you don't think you gonna care for the combat though, don't bother.

I have never had the need to force myself to finish a Pokemon game. This however was the first time. The lack of exploration, meaningful side quests and even the lackluster main story like failed to capture my interest. The only thing worthwhile about this game is the new megas and some new favorite characters. That's about it though. Probably one of the worst Pokemon games in the franchise.
If i had to make a list of the worst pokemon games ever made, this would be the second one right after sword and shield base game

Loved it. It was my first experience with pokemon since pokemon red that I played on a nokia N95 phone via emulator. SinceI got this pokemon, I can't swap the game for another on my switch 2, really excited to finish the game and try the online gameplay.
I love the combat changes, but I felt claustrophobic being trapped in the city forever.

First things first: if you like the typical Pokémon formula, you’re probably going to like this game. New trainer in an unfamiliar place, gets a starter Pokémon, goes off to explore — for the longest time that was enough for me. I never really minded the formula because it worked.

But even though this game technically follows the same structure, I couldn’t shake the feeling that something was off while playing. For me, Pokémon has always been about discovering new creatures and exploring a new world. And to be fair, the new Megas are pretty good, and the new battle system is genuinely amazing.

The exploration, though, is where it loses me. Even though the game takes place within a single city, the areas don’t feel varied at all. Interacting with Pokémon doesn’t feel natural the way it did in Arceus or Scarlet/Violet. Movement mechanics like jumping and dodging feel clunky. There are tons of missions, but they come across more like repetitive chores — especially the main ones — and the story does a pretty poor job of tying everything together.

I’m not really the type to complain about Pokémon games. I know I’ll always end up buying them. But this time, I decided not to get the DLC because the positives were heavily overshadowed by the negatives. Honestly, for the first time ever, I didn’t buy a Pokémon game the moment it was released.

I’m not trying to convince anyone not to buy it — like I said at the start, if you enjoy the traditional Pokémon formula, you’ll probably enjoy this game. What I am saying is that instead of having the fandom constantly split over whether it’s good or bad, we should stop turning a blind eye to the elements that clearly hold it back from being a great game.
Forget whatever the internet is yelling about — if you truly enjoy the franchise, go play Pokémon Legends: ZA. It’s easily the best Pokémon game ever made.

The new non turn-based gameplay is pretty fun and smooth, and the story is great and the characters are really enjoyable and interesting (I really love the characters and plot). It also has a good mix of options with gameplay going between fighting trainers, catching Pokémon, and fighting rouge mega evolved Pokémon. It’s also really fun seeing all the different new mega evolved Pokémon. 

Also all the side quests are all super fun and really different! They have you doing all kinds of different things with different little plots and some of them end up giving you a pokemon that’ll have a story behind it now! I love it!

The whole game does take place in one city, but it never really bothered me because there’s so much to do, and plenty of different looking areas to explore, and it being in just one city allowed them to spend time refining everything else.

Also also the final fight is by far the best a Pokémon game has ever had.

My only real critique is that the main story progression takes a little too long, but also I had fun the whole time so eh lol.

Another missed opportunity, and we'll definitely never get a good Pokémon game again, judging by people's complacency. The only positive thing is the gameplay. Those who actually say it has a good story and good characters clearly haven't played another JRPG in their lives. The first 25 hours are just repeating the same thing, with a practically nonexistent story and characters who at least aren't complete idiots. What pisses me off is that in the last 5 hours they actually tell you some interesting things, the characters are important, and there are even some cool cutscenes. And I ask myself... WHY THE HELL COULDN'T THE REST OF THE GAME BE LIKE THE LAST 5 HOURS? I'm so fed up with Nintendo, The Pokémon Company, and Game Freak. Oh, and I'll skip over how awful the game is because everyone sees it, but the fact that it still doesn't have voice acting in 2025 is a **** disgrace.

The beginning of the game, at least to me, was very fun. I love the change in the battle system, and the characters are enjoyable. However, the mid portion, and especially the post game is very lack luster. I wish there was more in the base game without a DLC. Otherwise its fun.
Pokemon fans complain about every game, maybe skip a game or two. This game is Fire  and I can’t put it down.

Game is an unoptimized mess, even on Switch 2 which shows how little effort they put into optimization 
The gameplay is mediocre, the story is good
For $70 you’d expect more since it’s the same price of games like Silent Hill or Battlefield 6
But you’re not getting that kind of quality from this game

Pokémon Legends ZA is a really great game. The new battle mechanic feels fresh and adds so much excitement. Visually it is the best-looking Pokémon game so far, even if some buildings and windows could use more detail. The only thing is the story can feel a bit repetitive at times, but overall it is a fun, engaging experience and a strong step forward for the series.

This game is enjoyable, and hits some marks for me as a fan of the previous legends game. However this game misses too many marks for me to give it higher than a 6/10. I would suggest people still play this game, but maybe when they are taking a break from other more enjoyable titles.

The gameplay loop in Pokemon Legends: Z-A is quite enjoyable. There are definitely some moments throughout the game that could’ve had some fluff trimmed off to condense the experience, but overall I had a great time. However, it is hard to overlook the graphical design choices in this game. They aren’t bad and visuals aren’t everything, but the immersion is constantly broken by the silly decisions made by GameFreak and Nintendo. They didn’t fully push me away, but I could see it being a huge issue for others. The world itself also feels dramatically small compared to what I expected. Even before unlocking anything in the game beyond what was already there at the start, I felt like I explored everything the game offered.

While the combat is a good step up from the standard (very fast and quick) the poor visuals are unexcusable.

A game from the #1 franchise in profit should NOT look this bad.
All the textures of the building have no tesselation, no reflections of the sun etc.
Even certain areas, that are not part of the open city, look way better than all of the outside.

Do not support these garbage.

Very fun enjoyable game. Is it the best Pokemon game? No, but i feel it's overhated. Many people hate the buildings because they're flat, but if they were detailed, auto glide would be way too OP, if they made it detailed auto glide spamming could break the exploration of the game, and if they set barriers people would get mad for crashing into nothing. Gamefreak's Legends series is one of the most innovative things they've made, nothing feels old. Sure it's annoying that the game is only in Lumiose city, but you still get 20 wild zones and there is always people around making it feel alive and like a real city. I think the story is very well executed, of course there are some things i don't like with the game but most of it is minor things. The charecters are good too, i feel that Pokemon Legends ZA has been criticized by the people who always put pressure on game Studios to always meet up with expectations, this game is highly enjoyable. Loved it 9/10.

This is a fine example of why user review scores are reactionary and untrustworthy. Legends Z-A is a great Pokémon game, with fresh combat, fun exploration, and a charming story. It doesn't look great visually, but visuals aren't everything. Likely my favorite Pokémon release since ORAS.

Pokemon Legends ZA is a great demonstration of Game Freak's capability and decision making. Their choice to commit to their strengths paid dividends, even if the environment holds back a greater adventure. Its easy to appreciate a game where everything comes together, but recognizing the limits, and choosing to make the best experience possible within those constraints also matters.	'Gameplay first' is the Nintendo mantra, and remaining true to that philosophy has made Legends ZA a worthy follow up to the groundbreaking Legends Arceus. Though there is still work ahead to fully realize the potential of the franchise, Game Freak and Pokemon fans can be proud of this stride forward.

Pokemon Legends Z-A is a good game in my opinion. While it does have its short comings such as the flat buildings, it is overall enjoyable. The story, soundtrack, designs, and dialogue are all quite good, and the gameplay is fantastic.

It feel like it's able to show you what the anime has been doing for years as a playable game. It starts off slow in the early game, but becomes a lot quicker as the game progresses, leading to the game being very fun. It has room for improvements such as the lack of abilities, but it's still good, which speaks to the game's quality overall.

An unexpectedly good game. Despite what everyone seems to be saying about the graphics, they really don't impact on the experience much, at least for me. I'm no professional critic and I'm just basing this review on how fun the game was to play. The characters are SO well developed, the new megas are (mostly) cool and the gameplay arc is strangely fun and addictive, making you want to keep playing. The combat was an interesting new twist and definitely freshened up the usual formula. Even after finishing the main story I still wanted to keep playing, something that I don't always want to do after a Pokémon game. This game was a truly enjoyable experience and fully worth the money paid, at least on the switch 2. I continue to be surprised that people are willing to rate this game so lowly simply because the buildings are not high definition. This is hardly an important aspect of the game and I know it's really important to some people but don't let that put you off from a genuinely fun game.

pokemon-scarlet.review: Good game, I think the main story of the three paths could have been denser, especially the main one about the Pokémon League. The post-game story and the DLCs are good, I think they could have gone even deeper into the topic of paradoxes, like including them more in the main story, not just in the **** addition, they could have “locked” some areas so that it wouldn't be so easy to break the game. Something I'd like to see in the future, if they decide to keep the world open, is for trainers and gym leaders to have flexible teams based on your medals. That way, you could almost decide your path, with certain limitations, of course, such as giving you the freedom to explore the first four areas and go in whatever order you want for the first four gyms, and so on.Finally, improve the graphics. I'm not asking to be able to see the characters' sweat, but I would like to see better-constructed landscapes, with little or no popping. and something they absolutely must focus on in future games is that areas with wild Pokémon should feel more alive, not just with Pokémon walking or flying around, but doing different things, just like in the anime, with Pokémon interacting with the environment, and each Pokémon with its own nature changing depending on your behavior, as well as other trainers. It would be interesting to go along the routes and see NPCs trying to catch a Pokémon or training.

I believe that this game has better gameplay than sword and shield, but that sword and shield has the superior story. Both, however, pale in comparison to Sun and moon.
I think these games get a bad rep from modern consumers being overly critical and negative without playing the game to enjoy, but to rage at it. The current user score is a good example of people not having reigns over their emotions. P.S. Nemona is a freaking joke

I have never played a game that lifeless before. Compared to the old DS pokemon games, this does not even feel like playing pokemon anymore. Annoying that you had to update it so often, just for it to somewhat work more or less. Personally, I'm beyond disappointed.

I didn't like it.A very buggy game in terms of graphics.Horrible for A modern game of Pokémon Which saves a **** tera form that can implement some legal strategies Apart from that No  I wouldn't recommend it to anyone.

This game is an embarrassment for a franchise that once defined handheld RPGs. On the Nintendo Switch — a console that has delivered technical marvels like Breath of the Wild and Super Mario Odyssey — this game looks and runs like a rushed beta.Performance: Constant frame drops, glitches, and visual bugs plague the experience. The open world is riddled with pop-in, broken textures, and laughably poor animations. It feels unfinished, as if Game Freak had no idea how to develop for modern hardware.Graphics: Bland environments, flat lighting, and character models that look outdated even by 3DS standards. The Switch has proven it can handle vast, beautiful worlds, but Scarlet settles for mediocrity.Gameplay: While the open-world concept had potential, it’s wasted on repetitive objectives, shallow exploration, and a lack of meaningful progression. The “freedom” is an illusion — the game funnels you into predictable battles and fetch quests.Story & Characters: Forgettable, uninspired, and padded with dialogue that adds nothing. Rivals and NPCs are caricatures, offering no emotional weight or memorable arcs.Overall Polish: Scarlet feels like a cash grab, banking on the Pokémon brand rather than delivering a product worthy of the Switch era.Verdict: Pokémon Scarlet is the worst kind of disappointment — a flagship title that fails to meet even basic standards of quality. On a console capable of breathtaking experiences, Scarlet stands out as a shameful reminder of how far the series has fallen.

It is decent if you play it on Switch 2, if you don't, it's a disgrace. The story is very fun, the graphics are bad but at least colorful, having an open world pokemon game is great but the world is also kind of empty except for the creatures.
Thanks to the Switch 2, it's now perfectly playable, now you can enjoy how bad it is at 60 FPS. And even then, it doesn't run perfectly in Area 0. What a terrible game.

I always thought I loved the Pokémon franchise, but Pokémon Scarlet showed me that wasn't quite the case. The game starts out interesting, the first steps give the impression that it's going to be something special. But when you compare the "open world" with Pokémon Legends: Arceus, it becomes clear that Scarlet is worse, even being the most **** addition, the game has many bugs and technical problems. A very hollow and grotesque experience. An amorphous collection of bile masquerading as a piece of entertainment software. It is as if a misshapen lump of digital flesh gained the ability to hate. Despite its clear disdain for the mortal coil along with everything good and holy in this world, it still struggles along, twisting and pulsating to maintain circulation and erratically convulsing past any brief respite of respiratory arrest. It has no mouth yet it must scream.

The technical side is completely outdated—it honestly feels like playing a PS2 game. The level design is awful, with a huge but empty and confusing map. Dungeons are basically nonexistent, just simple corridors. Pokémon are ridiculously small, so you spend more time watching your feet than exploring the world, which you’ll never really remember anyway. The Sierra Napada area is terrible, a real insult to players. Cities are totally empty, and NPCs have nothing interesting to say. The camera control is horrible. Most battles end up with the camera clipping through the ground, walls, or simply missing half of the Pokémon on screen. The lack of voice acting is becoming more and more painful, especially during key story moments. The idea of having three main questlines is nice, but it’s ruined by the lack of automatic level scaling. You can easily end up fighting a gym leader with level 20 Pokémon when your team is already level 50. Gym challenges are extremely dumb—the Psychic-type gym is probably the worst example. Characters are cute and well designed, but there are no side quests or events to interact with them, so you never get attached to anyone like in older games. The teachers are a bit more present since you attend classes and can talk to them between lessons—my favorite is Guaro. Pokémon models look bad; you can literally see the polygons. The new Terastalization mechanic is awful, especially compared to old systems like Gigamax or Mega Evolutions. The game is way too easy and lacks any difficulty settings. The idea of giving you the legendary Pokémon at the start is nice, but it’s not enough to save the experience. The post-game is almost nonexistent—basically locked behind paid DLC. In short: a game with some good ideas, but poorly executed in every way.

Okay, I have to be honest. I'm sending my copy of Pokemon Scarlet and Violet back. As a long-time fan, it's a real ****'s talk about the technical stuff first because you can't ignore it. This game is rough. I'm not just talking about the graphics looking a bit dated, I'm talking about the game slowing down constantly in the open world, especially around the lake. I had Pokemon and trees pop into existence right in front of me all the time. And the bugs... I once got stuck inside a rock and had to fast travel out. Another time, the game just froze during a picnic. It feels less like I'm playing a finished game and more like I'm testing a broken early **** the world itself feels so empty and lazy. Take the big main city, Mesagoza. It's huge, but it's all for show. You can't go into almost any of the buildings. Every street looks the same. Compare that to a place like Goldenrod City from the Gold and Silver days, which felt alive with a department store, a radio tower, the Magnet Train station. Mesagoza is just a pretty backdrop, and a repetitive one at that. The open world is just a bunch of empty fields. Where's the clever level design like the twisty paths of Mt. Coronet from Diamond and Pearl?But what really gets me is how the stories are told. The game has some serious moments, especially with Arven and his sick dog, Mabosstiff. But having these emotional scenes play out with just text boxes and characters staring blankly at each other is just awkward. It completely kills the mood. Games from the Nintendo DS era like Pokemon Black and White had way more compelling stories and characters without needing voice acting, but Scarlet and Violet's dialogue feels flat and forgettable in **** just feels like the developers cut every corner they could. For the most successful media franchise on the planet, this is not good enough. We've seen them make beautiful, polished games with heart on much weaker consoles. Playing this just makes me feel like they don't think we deserve better.

I have a lot of problems with this game, and I believe most of it starts with throwing away the things that sword and shield did right. The art style went from anime style that fit Pokémon to a dull color palette and uncanny semirealism that makes the protagonists look like porcelain dolls. The customization nose dived going from fun outfits with cute skirts, hats, heart pupils and vibrant hair colors that let me express the fairy type trainer I am, to now suddenly losing my individuality with a schooluniform and dozens of helmets. The world also suffers. Most of would is just empty grass lands with nothing noteworthy. No magical mushroom forests, volcanic caverns, desert dunes, ruins, reefs, large rivers. There is also severe Lack of environmental features like bushes, boulders; flowers, tall grass, trees and everything else. Most of the buildings you can’t interact with and half the ones you can you can’t enter and pull up menus instead. Lastly I find team stars story to drag the game down. The message is preachy with unsatisfying conclusion. It doesn’t help that this was the third playground bully team in row. 

A Pokémon game is about going on an adventure and most the aspects that immerse the player were neglected and team star as antagonist did nothing to push our imagination.

Pokemon Yellow Version taught me how to read when I was 4 years old. Since then, I've played and beaten every single mainline game in the franchise, even going as far as completing the pokedex in many of them. That being said, Pokemon's attempt at an Open World adventure was such a massive letdown that I wonder if they were even trying at all. Pokemon Scarlet's open world lacks a genuine effort at world design or world building. There's a complete lack of environmental storytelling and nothing makes this more obvious than the fact that you can't go inside almost any of the buildings across the map. Meaningful NPC interactions are absent almost entirely. Open world games should reward exploration, something that this game fails tremendously at considering the open world is almost entirely empty. There are few-if-any dynamic events out in the world, which makes it feel bland and dead. The game's performance from a technical perspective was embarrassing at launch and is only remedied by purchasing the Switch 2, an entirely new console. The artwork from a visual perspective was heavily lacking. The game attempted a "realism" approach which made the pokemon look okay compared to Sword and Shield, but the rest of the world still looks awful. There's a clear lack of identity in the game's aesthetic -- it looks like almost every other 3D open world RPG on a mobile device, which is a stark contrast from games like BotW which have a unique aesthetic that works well on the Switch. The narrative direction was weak -- the Gym challenges were average at best but at least they were familiar. The Herba Mystica quest line was solid but the Team Star quest line was embarrassingly shallow and unrewarding. The story shines in Area Zero, but the fact that this is the end of the game means most people will have put it down way before then. The only things giving this game any points at all are the sound design, some decent new pokemon designs, and the Area Zero story line. Pokemon is the highest-grossing media franchise in the world... How they could have any dignity after releasing this slop is beyond me. In fact, the Switch as a console generation was tremendously embarrassing for Pokemon, so let's hope they can pick up the pieces before it's too late.

Pokemon games are very hard to rate because they've never really added anything super major or insanely exciting with each iteration. And they haven't really been the best games from an objective standpoint for a long time now. But, they are still, somehow, probably the best monster tamer games on the market. It's really surprising and kind of sad that all the other ones are so mediocre they can't even come close to a series that keeps rehashing the same game over and over again. But that's the sad world us monster tamer fans must live in.

If there was any monster tamers out there that remotely came close to Pokemon, Scarlet/Violet would be an easy 5. But since there's not, you kinda gotta give it a 7. Just because if you want a monster tamer, it's really the only game in town. Even now in 2025 and likely for the foreseeable future unless some studio shocks us and makes a Pokemon killer one day. 

But as for Scarlet/Violet, sandwiches are cool. I wish Pokemon were harder to catch but with the number of Pokemon now hitting way over 1,000, it's fully understandable why they're becoming easier and easier to catch. Because I put 150 hours into Violet and I only have 1/3 of all Pokemon. Also, Wonder Trade and Hacked Pokemon is what killed anything besides collecting Pokemon in the mainline Pokemon games. You can't trade with literally anyone online except real life friends who you know for a fact didn't hack pokemon or trade with strangers online. And battling is a complete joke as well as online trading because they're both filled with nothing but computer generated fake pokemon. So the only thing worth doing in mainline Pokemon games is slowly trying to catch em' all by yourself like I do or with trusted friends who you know don't trade online, hack or cheat. 

SCORE: 7 (Good) Only because there's literally no other monster tamers on the market worth playing.

Pokemon Scarlet embodies every mistake that an open world game can make. The world is technically open from the start, but freedom to explore does little when the world is empty and there is not much to do, no interesting side quest, no proper interactive NPCs, no interesting landmarks to explore. During the game you hear about cool places that the region have, but when arriving to those places you surely end up being disappointed due the ugly and uninspiring reality. Similarly towns you arrive during the game all feel unoriginal without any specific identity to them. All buildings are copy pasted and NPCs don´t have any interesting dialogue making towns feel really lifeless. In all honestly the game lacks any incentive to go beyond the set path. For the biggest region of the series Paldea feels really small and uninteresting.

Not to mention the lack of difficulty scaling for challenges. First time in the series you have a possibility to combat gyms in any order, which sounds good on paper, but outside specific challenges, player is incentivized to go specific path or end up being extremely over-or underleveled. The game doesn´t give any hints on the difficulty of each challenge either making anyone wanting to go in optimal order to rely on external information. The game being really easy doesn´t help either. While no one expects Pokemon to be difficult, Scarlet takes it one step further. If the player does anything extra between challenges like catching pokemon, battling trainers or exploring the region they inevitably end up being easily overleveled making the fighting really boring, again disincentivizing exploration if wanting any form of challenge.

All in all, Pokemon Scarlet feel like a linear Pokemon game with tech demo open world plastered to it in the late stages of development. It misses every point which makes an open world interesting and honestly would make much better game were it truly made to be linear without the illusion of freedom the half baked open world offers. 

For such an empty world it would at least be expected for it to work properly, but the performance is honestly atrocious and having stable 30fps is a rare occurrence. That could make sense if the game would have a lot going for it, but graphical fidelity and the art style being mediocre at best and draw distance being really bad don´t give any excuses for why it cannot perform at least decently.

Only saving grace for the game is the final area and the story contained there but you have to suffer through the rest of the game making it not really worth the wait. Otherwise the gameplay is exactly as expected from previous games and while not horrible itself, it does little to innovate the stale formula. Over all Pokemon Scarlet is a husk of an open world with no reason of exploration having implemented all of the problems of previous instalments and criticisms of open world formula without offering any solutions to them. As a long time Pokemon fan I am extremely disappointed especially after the previous game; Pokemon Legends Arceus that showed a lot of potential.

This is an "open world" game where you choose your treasure hunt, but stop as soon as you deviate from the intended path the entire game falls apart. If you don't follow the hollow intended path be prepared to be wildly overleveled without doing any autobattles.

I only engaged in pokemon fights when I saw a pokemon I hadn't caught yet, sometimes I'd KO by mistake but even this being the case I was consistently very overleveled for tasks in the game. You cannot choose your own adventure, you cannot ignore a certain part of the game say team star then return to it later with proper scaling and get any value out of it. The game makes the critical mistake of allowing you to enter kitakami right from the beginning which if you choose to pursue will destroy any balancing in the game. The game will sometimes surprise you with impossible battles such as the ogre clan and you just have to suffer slowly losing all your pokemon till you run out because the game has no sort of balancing what so ever.

By far the worst pokemon experience I've ever had and close to one of the worse game experiences I've ever had.

I like that tall grass is gone and you choose your battles, the graphics are ok infact character assets generally look pretty good it's the copy and paste assets like trees, buildings, and grass that they decided to skimp on maybe for the sake of performance but none the less. Dynamic battle engagement is cool but so often clips the camera into or under the environment before/after battles.

The biggest issue is the progression and lack of scaling, especially for a game that tauts itself as you being able to go on your own "treasure hunt" (their words) and yet you are punished severely for deviating and it can be game ruining. 

The whole experience feels generally empty, meaningless, and not an adventure. I miss nurse joy and the pokemart too, they don't give the "gas stops" any character, they feel empty and meaningless like the rest of this game. I miss entering someone's home and talking bout what's on TV, their favorite pokemon, trading pokemon, hearing town news, etc. Why do they have buildings if no one is ever in them?

I can only recommend this game to people who are able to tolerate constant frame drops, technical issues, and can make their own fun.

The best moments of scarlet and violet come towards the end. The games have awful performance and issues that make them unenjoyable until the story picks up enough momentum to be interesting. The open world itself is only useful for encountering wild pokemon but the encounters were handled better in Legends Arceus by the active capture system. 

Buy this if you like playing online as the Terastal mechanic is extremely fun to use, otherwise avoid it as the single player is extremely extremely disappointing.
The lowest point of Pokémon games. It lags, it bugs, graphic is even worse than Arceus (far worse). It'sa pity because story could have been a good 8. Too broke to consider good points.
The worst experience in games of all my life. I want my money back and i never will buy a Pokémon game again. I lot of bugs.
Is not a terrible game, but there are problems like bugs, glitches and low frame rate, i hope that Game Freak learned from this.

While the performance issues are very hard to forgive, they are far from the biggest detractor for the ninth generation of Pokémon. As a matter of fact, I propose a very strange idea. The biggest detractor of this game is every other Pokémon game on the Switch.

Every aspect of this game is a step back. On its own merit, it would be good. Not great, not excellent, but good. However, with a world that feels emptier than Legends: Arceus, raids that feel worse than those in Sword/Shield, and a story that has been completely disjointed (as compared to any of the previous generations) due to a lack of controlled pacing both in terms of gameplay and writing, there is nothing here that doesn't feel worse than something. Given that the core fanbase consists of longtime players, this comparison is unavoidable, even on a massive scale.

That said, there is merit to the game. A cast of new mons that, personally, I enjoy, and methods of shiny hunting being more accessible than ever means that this game hardly felt like a slog for many hours. However, it is undeniable that the quality has slipped, and for any new fans, I will definitely continue to direct them to the previous generation.

Pokemon Scarlet and Violet had the potential to be the best mainline Pokemon games in the entire franchise thus far. However, due to extremely poor performances issues, a rushed and unpolished world, as well as the lack of general care of the state the game was released in, they end up currently being the worst the series has to offer. 

Don't get me wrong, I love Pokemon! They did improve upon a lot with these games. For example, the Tera phenomenon was very interesting to play with, added new elements to the game, and really shook things up for both Casual and Competitive players. Having an open world, as well as three different stories was also a great addition as it deviates from the traditional Pokemon formula we have had for the past 20 years. Unfortunately, due to the lack of time being put into the game, the open world ends up still being linear since the lack of level scaling makes you play the game in order or ascending levels, instead of being able to truly choose your own adventure.

As of writing this, the game has yet to have had a meaningful patch fixing any of these issues, so more than likely the game will continue to exist in the awful state it has been in since launch. It is quite frankly unacceptable for The Pokemon Company to release a game in this state, as they the highest-grossing media franchise to exist in the entire world, and definitely have the financial resources to create higher quality games then they have here.

I personally will not be supporting The Pokemon Company's games anymore until the they begin to release them in a finished state, and would advise others who truly care about the health of current and future Pokemon games to do the same. Thank you.

I played Pokemon Scarlet and I have to say, that I liked many things in there. The new functions like the portable boxes and the new Pokemon Centers maked the gameplay faster and stopped tieback and forth running, while training pokemon. Also I liked the storyline, even if the Characters were characterless and monotonous. But now to the negative points. The world feels really unfinished and empty. Often there are big empty areas with some trees or rocks. Nothing really special. Also the towns are boring, because you cant go in the houses anymore and most of the characters are unnecessary. No cool side quests, no little rewarding. Everything is pointless and unnecessary and I had less reason to explore the world than in previous parts of the series.I like the new way of an open world pokemon, but everything feels scripted and characterless. You can do better GameFreak!

Open world concept sure, it was interesting,otherwise, abysmal, bad dialogue, gym mini games that where nonsensical and idiotic, a rival that is beyond annoying, a pokemon academy that makes no sense and overall a dull and mediocre story. This game has nothing of the fun simple pokemon we grew up with.

Pokémon Scarlet and Violet is a mixed bag to say the least. While is had many fun ideas, interesting concepts, and possibly the best writing in the whole series, it’s impossible to ignore the train wreck that is this game visuals and performance. The game runs ok at best, and it barely runs at all at worst. And improvements made to textures are impossible to see with all the pixelation on display. (It’s also worth mentioning that the animations, were improved in some ways in this title, but are still a notable step back from Legends Arceus) I want to love Scarlet and Violet, but I can’t. Let’s hope that Game Freak can learn from this moving forward.
Fun to play but if you want a well-polished Pokémon game without bugs, don't buy this game, Honestly Over-hated though.

Some questionable creative decisions. The choice to commit to a fully open world map advanced the series but at the cost of performance. The graphics are pretty poor and then you get into the frame drops and other visual bugs. Some of the new pokedex entries are completely mailed in where others are going to be favourites for coming generations.

I really liked the storyline in this series. I know, I know, there are some strong technical issues, but I didn't experience them with the handheld switch. I honestly didn't mind the direction they went with this one and really do think the storyline was borderline cinematic.
The new pokemon are all so adorable and the the new features are great welcome to the series

I kinda went into this game knowing about things like it's performance issues, and to be honest, that's my only major downside with this (aside from the lackluster story, but that's an issue with most modern Pokemon Games, so I stopped caring about the story as much and started expecting lackluster stories). This game is genuinely the most fun I've ever had with a Pokemon game, and it's all because of the open world aspect of it. It feels so free, it feels like a real adventure, and the fact that there are rarely any hard roadblocks keeping you out of specific areas (as in unnatural ones, like a random herd of Psyduck blocking the way to a city) makes it feel that much more free. This also kind of helps with the issue of difficulty, but not really. Difficulty almost kept this from getting an 8, but I found that the lack of difficulty was kind of balanced out by how fun it was, so it barely made it. I didn't find a lot of unique strategies used by trainers, because the closest thing to a strategy someone would have is something to counter 1 super-effective type, which is more than some games have, but still... would've liked to have seen more use of held items. And I can't ignore the performance issues. The game can go below 20 fps sometimes, but mostly, it handles 30 to 40FPS well... that is, unless you go in a more crowded area. The crowded areas are so laggy, and I think it's a byproduct of the graphics, which are gorgeous, but gorgeous in the way a poisonous plant can be; yeah, it's pretty to look at... but once it does stuff, it ain't gonna be pretty, that's for sure. Sure, you can just play it on a stronger platform, but I don't know if Nintendo's next console will be backward compatible with the Switch, and if it is, I don't know how much more bearable it can make the lag, but still. I would've given this a 9... but the lackluster story already barred it from a 10, and the performance issues were major enough to knock it down to an 8. I also don't wanna count DLC into this review, but if I did, then this would be a 9. The DLC is so good, but we're talking about the main game. It's the most fun Pokemon game so far, but it's also the worst performing (as in how it runs), and probably the only game where I've ever wanted the graphics to be worse so that I don't have to panic about the game crashing whenever I enter an area with 8 or more breathing organisms. Performance issues aside, this game's fun. I would recommend if you can either handle lag, or if you know of a way to run the game on something stronger.

The game itself is really good! I love the open world aspect and the story and characters, for a Pokemon game, are incredible. However, it's hard to overlook the graphical issues and the lack of level scaling makes it really hard to enjoy the open world. This is a step in the right direction for the Pokemon franchise, but if they really want to tap into the open world thing they have a lot of things to improve on.

I cannot tell if im getting older or Pokemon games are just getting worse. These games are not only easy, but the quality of game that Nintendo can put out is pitifully bellow where it should be. This game made me sad, because as someone that has played just about every single pokemon game up until this point, this one I think finally made me quit. Unless I decide to come back to VGC, pokemon is dead to me... however a good game is all they need to release to get me to come back, but looking at their track record... I doubt that any time soon -_-

If the game hadn't been so unpolished, the score would have been a 9. The visuals are directly from the N64, and the flaws and glitches are really bothersome. But my assessment **** is dependent on how much fun and excitement I have while playing it. In my view, I prefer fun over the graphics. For that reason, I thought this game was close to a 9. It is most likely the Pokemon game with the most potential and was among the finest in terms of content. The game may have been a masterpiece if it had been more well-polished. Nintendo should rebuild this game, improving the graphics and, most importantly, addressing all the faults and errors. If they did that, it would probably be one of the best pokemon games of all time.

I'm a long time Pokemon fan. This Game is verry fun and I have more than 100 hours in this game. AlthoughI I had so much fun, the graffics are terrible and often I just got stuck in the air for 6 seconds. And I'm not even going to start talking about the frame rate.

Although the graphics are worse than the other Pokémon games on switch, they do improve lots of Pokémon models and animations. The story is the best we’ve seen and battles, especially late game can get fairly difficult. Frame rate wasn’t much of an issue for me. Open world is a bit empty and it feels like you have to constantly fly from one side of the map to the other to follow a logical level progression, which doesn’t feel right. But as usual, great new set of mons, amazing soundtrack, and fun gameplay.

I DON'T CARE! I will say it I don't care what people think. Compared to Pokémon Sword and Shield Pokémon Scarlet and Violet are top tier games! I'll say it here anyone who here has played pixelated Pokémon Yellow, Red, Blue, you lot have no right to complain. You have played pixelated low fps Pokémon before.

Pokémon Scarlet and Violet have some of the best Storytelling and best characters to date! I have played through both Scarlet and Violet multiple times and have encountered no big issues or glitches. Not only that but it's the only other game that I have actually completed the Pokedex for! I only put effort into the Pokedex if I truly enjoy a Pokémon game to the fullest!

I didn't complete the Alola dex, but that's because, one, version exclusive Pokémon, two, no one to trade with. The only other game that I completed the Pokedex in is Legends Arceus! The characters are amazing and the stories are breathtaking and I say Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, as of writing this are the best Pokémon games on the Nintendo Switch!
The game is great and it makes fun the only 2 Things i have to say are it get's boring if you play to long and the graphics aren't the best but the music and everything Else is really good

( I played this game a bit after the actual release so my opinion is on the later versions). Pokémon scarlet/violet is generally a good game. It is definitely a great step into the right direction for future Pokémon games. Its open world is very expansive but has some flaws. When I was playing the game, I didn’t notice any major problems aside from some slight frame drops like in the large lake area in the north west . Even after completing the game and finishing the Pokédex, I didn’t have any visual or physical bugs aside from two small glitches, which didn’t affect my gameplay. While yes, the open world has flaws, I think it’s still amazing.  The story is nothing to scoff at as well, the characters are interesting and refreshing. The story keeps my attention and makes me actually want to see what happens next. I understand why it was released when it was, but I feel that Nintendo should just take their time to make games at full potential. I hope to see great things from the Nintendo company.

I rated this game an 8/10 because although the game is very fun and entertaining, there are still bugs in places. I didn’t get the game as soon as it came out, so I was not a part of the group of players who had to enjoy the game while it was virtually unplayable due to massive glitches, bugs, and extreme lag. Though even after many bug fixes, it does still lag a bit under certain conditions but not to the point where you cannot work around it. I have 200+ hours in the game after finishing it in its entirety and sometimes the lag in specific areas still annoys me but I have been thoroughly pleased by the outcome and the DLC only adds to that delight. Being only the second game I’ve owned of the franchise and coming after what I've heard be considered a groundbreaking approach to the game, I was still extremely satisfied.

Honestly I can see why lots of people hate this game, but at the same time I absolutely loved it, from start to finish, it was the first game that replayed more than once, it keeps you locked on the scree, it's the first game that made me want to complete the Pokéde, the 3 stories we're top 3 of the franchise, there were lots of technical issues but sometimesi didn't even felt them, the Music was awesome and some parts of the map(I don't particularly like it) are beautiful.
I hink haven't had a good experience witha Pokémon games since Black and White 2, this game made me fell in love again withthe franchise and even with all their faults I had an incredible experiencez there are som many things to do and now it's bug free, so you can play it really well, the only thing is the fps drops but it's not that big of a deal, I’ll always wish that Game Freak had taken another year or maybe even just a few more months to polish it enough to be playablefrom the start.
Despite graphical and perfromance issues (which was never the focus of the series) these games are the most fun I had with Pokemon in a long time.
